Not to afford; to withhold. DEN Y. Who find not Providence all good and wise, DEN Y. Alike in what it gives, and what denies? DEN Y.
To disown; to refuse or neglect to acknowledge; not to confess.
To reject; to disown; not to receive or embrace. DEN Y. He hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel. 7 Timothy 5:8. Denying ungodliness and worldly lusts. Titus 2:72.
deobstruct: To remove obstructions, or impediments to a passage; to clear from any thing that hinders the passage of fluids in the proper ducts of the body; as, to deobstruct the pores or lacteals.
deobstruent: Removing obstructions; having power to clear or open the natural ducts of the fluids and secretions of the body; resolving viscidities; aperient.
Any medicine which removes obstructions and opens the natural passages of the fluids of the body, as the pores and lacteal vessels; an aperient Calomel is a powerful deobstruent.
deodand: In England, a personal chattel which is the immediate occasion of the death of a rational creature, and for that reason, given to God, that is, forfeited to the king, to be applied to pious uses, and distributed in alms by his high almoner. Thus, if a cart runs over a man and kills him, the cart is forfeited as a deodand.
deoppilate: To free from obstructions; to clear a passage.
deoxydate: To deprive of oxygen, or reduce from the state of an oxyd.
deosydated: Reduced from the state of an oxyd.
deoxydating: Reducing from the state of an oxyd.
deoxydation: The act or process of reducing from the state of an oxyd.
deoxygenation: The act or operation of depriving of oxygen.
depaint: To paint; to picture; to represent in colors, as by painting the resemblance.
depart: To go or move from. Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire. Matthew 25:41. It is followed by from, or from is implied before the place left. I will depart to my own land, that is, I will depart from this place to my own land. Vumbers 10:30.
To go from; to leave; to desist, as from a practice. Jehu departed not from the sins of Jeroboam. Jehoshaphat departed not from the way of Asa his father.
To leave; to deviate from; to forsake; not to adhere to or follow; as, we cannot depart from our rules. I have not departed from thy judgments. Psalm 119:102.
To desist; to leave; to abandon; as, he would not depart from his purpose, resolution, or demand.
To be lost; to perish; to vanish; as, his glory has departed.
departer: One who refines metals by separation.
departing: Going from; leaving; desisting; forsaking; vanishing; dying.
department: Literally, a separation or division; hence, a separate part, or portion; a division of territory; as the departments of France.

A separate allotment or part of business; a distinct province, in which a class of duties are allotted to a particular person; as the department of state, assigned to the secretary of state; the treasury department; the department of war.
A separate station; as, the admirals had their respective departments. Nearly in this sense, during war, were used in America, the terms, Northern and Southern departments .
departmental: Pertaining to a department, or division.
departure: The act of going away; a moving from or leaving a place; as a departure from London.
Death; decease; removal from the present life. The time of my departure is at hand.2 Timothy 4:6.
A forsaking; abandonment; as a departure from evil.
A desisting; as a departure from a purpose.
depasture: To feed; to graze. If a man takes in a horse, or other cattle, to graze and depasture in his grounds, which the law calls agistment- .
depauperate: To make poor; to impoverish; to deprive of fertility or richness; as, to depauperate the soil or the blood.
depend: To hang; to be sustained by being fastened or attached to something above; followed by from. From the frozen beard . Long icicles depend.

To be connected with anything, as the cause of its existence or of its operation and effects; to rely on; to have such connection with any thing as a cause, that without it, the effect would not be produced; followed by on or upon. We depend on God for existence; we depend on air for respiration; vegetation depends on heat and moisture; the infant depends on its parents for support; the peace of society depends on good laws and a. faithful administration.
To adhere; to hold to; to be retained.
To be in suspense; to be undetermined; as, the cause still depends. But the verb is seldom used in this sense. We use the participle; as, the suit is still depending in court.

To rely; to rest with confidence; to trust; to confide; to have full confidence or belief, we depend on the word or assurance of our friends. We depend on the arrival of the mail at the usual hour. Depend on it, the knave will deceive us. To depend on or upon, to rely; to trust in, with confidence.
dependable: That may be depended on; as dependable friendships.
dependence: A state of hanging down from a = supporter.
Any thing hanging down; a series of things hanging to another. And made a long dependence from the bough.

Concatenation; connection by which one thing is sustained by another, in its place, operations or effects, or is affected by it. But of this frame the bearings and the ties, t The strong connections, nice dependencies.

A state of being at the disposal of another; a state of being subject to the will of an intelligent cause, or to the power and operation of any other cause; inability to sustain itself without the aid of. We ought to feel our dependence on God for life and support. The child should be sensible of his dependence on his parents. In the natural and moral world, we observe the dependence of one thing on another.
Reliance; confidence; trust; a resting on; as, we may have a firm dependence on the promises of God.
dependency: A state of hanging down from a = supporter.
Any thing hanging down; a series of things hanging to another. And made a long dependence from the bough.

Concatenation; connection by which one thing is sustained by another, in its place, operations or effects, or is affected by it. But of this frame the bearings and the ties, t The strong connections, nice dependencies.

A state of being at the disposal of another; a state of being subject to the will of an intelligent cause, or to the power and operation of any other cause; inability to sustain itself without the aid of. We ought to feel our dependence on God for life and support. The child should be sensible of his dependence on his parents. In the natural and moral world, we observe the dependence of one thing on another.
Reliance; confidence; trust; a resting on; as, we may have a firm dependence on the promises of God.
dependent: Hanging down; as a dependent leaf. The furs in the tails were dependent.

Subject to the power of; at the disposal of; not able to exist or sustain itself without the will or power of. Thus, we are dependent on God and his providence; and effect may be dependent on some unknown cause.

Relying on for support or favor; unable to subsist or to perform any thing, without the aid of. Children are dependent on their parents for food and clothing. The pupil is dependent on his preceptor for instruction.
One who is at the disposal of another; one who is sustained by another, or who relies on another for support or favor; a retainer; as, the prince was followed by a numerous train of dependents.
depending: Hanging down; relying. 2 .a. Pending; undecided; as a suit or question.
deperdit: That which is lost or destroyed.
dephlegmate: To deprive of superabundant water, as by evaporation or distillation, used of spirit and acids; to clear spirit or acids of aqueous matter; to rectify.
dephlegmation: The operation of separating water from spirits and acids, by evaporation or repeated distillation; called also concentration, particularly when acids are the subject.
dephlegmedness: A state of being freed from water.
dephlogisticate: To deprive of phlogiston, or the supposed principle of inflammability.
dephlogisticated: Deprived of phlogiston. Dephlogisticated air, is an elastic fluid capable of supporting animal life and flame much longer than common air. It is now called oxygen, oxygen gas, or vital air.
depict: To paint; to portray; to form a likeness in colors; as, to depict a lion on a shield.
To describe; to represent in words; as, the poet depicts the virtues of his hero in glowing language.
depicting: Painting; representing in colors, or in words.
depicture: To paint; to picture; to represent in colors.
depilation: The act of pulling off the hair.
depilatory: Having the quality or power to take off hair and make bald.
Any application which is used to take off the hair of an animal body; such as lime and orpiment.
deplantation: The act of taking up plants from beds.
depletion: The act of emptying; particularly, in the medical art, the act of diminishing the quantity of blood in the vessels by venesection; bloodletting.
deplorable: That may be deplored or lamented; lamentable; that demands or causes lamentation; hence, sad; calamitous; grievous; miserable; wretched; as, the evils of life are deplorable; the Pagan world is ina deplorable condition. Deplorate, in a like sense, is not used.
In popular use, low; contemptible; pitiable; as deplorable stupidity.
deplorableness: The state of being deplorable; misery; wretchedness; a miserable state.
deplorably: In a manner to be deplored; lamentably; miserable; as, manners are deplorably corrupt.
deploration: The act of lamenting. In music, a dirge or mournful strain.
deplore: To lament; to bewail; to mourn; to feel or express deep and poignant grief for. We deplored the death of Washington.
deplorer: One who deplores, or deeply laments; a deep mourner.
deploy: To display; to open; to extend; a military term.
To open; to extend; to form a more extended front or line.
deplumation: The stripping or falling off of plumes or feathers.
A tumor of the eye-lids with loss of hair.<
deplume: To strip or pluck off feathers; to deprive of plumage.
depone: To lay down as a pledge; to wage.
deponent: A deponent verb, in the Latin Grammar, is a verb which has a passive termination, with an active signification, and wants one of the passive participles; as, loquor, to speak.

One who deposes, or gives a deposition under oath; one who gives written testimony to be used as evidence in a court of justice. With us in New England, this word is never used, I believe, for a witness who gives oral testimony in court. In England, a deponent is one who gives answers under oath to interrogatories exhibited in chancery.
depopulate: To dispeople; to unpeople; to deprive of inhabitants, whether by death, or by expulsion. It is not synonymous with laying waste or destroying, being limited to the loss of inhabitants; as, an army or a famine may depopulate a country. It rarely expresses an entire loss of inhabitants, but often a great diminution of their numbers. The deluge nearly depopulated the earth.
depopulation: The act of dispeopling; destruction or expulsion of inhabitants.
depopulator: One who depopulates; one who destroys or expels the inhabitants of a city, town or country; a dispeopler.
deport: With the reciprocal pronoun, to carry; to demean; to behave. Let an embassador deport himself in the most graceful manner before a prince.
To transport; to carry away, or from one country to another. He told us, he had been deported to Spain, with a hundred others like himself.
Behavior; carriage; demeanor; deportment; as goddess-like deport.
deportation: Transportation; a carrying away; a removal from one country to another, or to a distant place; exile; banishment.
deporting: Carrying away; removing to a distant place or country; transporting; banishing.
deportment: Carriage; manner of acting in relation to the duties of life; behavior; demeanor; conduct; management.
deposable: That may be deposed, or deprived of office.
deposal: The act of deposing, or divesting of office.
depose: To lay down; to throw; to let fall; as, the flood deposed fine particles of earth on the bank of the river. In this sense, we now use deposit.
To reduce from a throne or other high station; to dethrone; to degrade; to devest of office; as, to depose a king or a pope.
To give testimony on oath, especially to give testimony which is committed to writing; to give answers to interrogatories, intended as evidence in a court.
To take away; to strip; to divest.
deposer: One who deposes or degrades from office.
deposit: To lay down; to lay; to throw down. A crocodile deposits her eggs in the sand. A bird deposits eggs in a nest. An inundation deposits particles of earth on a meadow.
To lay up; to lay in a place for preservation. We deposit the produce of the earth in barns, cellars of storehouses. We deposit goods in a warehouse, and books in a library.

To lodge in the hands of a person for safe-keeping or other purpose; to commit to the care of; to entrust; to commit to one as a pledge. We say, the bond is deposited in the hands of an attorney; money is deposited as a pledge, or security: .
depositary: A person with whom any thing is left or lodged in trust; one to whom a thing is committed for safe keeping, or to be used for the benefit of the owner; a trustee; a guardian. The Jews were the depositaries of the sacred writings.
In law, one to whom goods are bailed to be kept for the bailor without a recompense.
deposition: The act of laying or throwing down; as, soil is formed by the deposition of fine particles, during a flood.
That which is thrown down; that which is lodged; as, banks are sometimes depositions of alluvial matter.
The act of giving testimony under oath.
The attested written testimony of a witness; an affidavit.

The act of dethroning a king, or the degrading of a person from an office or station; a divesting of sovereignty, or of office and dignity; a depriving of clerical orders. A deposition differs from abdication; an abdication being voluntary, and a deposition.
depository: A place where any thing is lodged for safe-keeping. A warehouse is a depository for goods; a clerks office, for records.
depravation: The act of making bad or worse; the act of corrupting.

The state of being made bad or worse; degeneracy; a state in which good qualities are lost, or impaired. We speak of the depravation of morals, manners or goverment; of the heart or mind; of nature, taste.
deprave: To make bad or worse; to impair good qualities; to make bad qualities worse; to vitiate; to corrupt; as, to deprave manners, morals, government, laws; to deprave the heart, mind, will, understanding, taste, principles.
depraved: Made bad or worse; vitiated; tainted; corrupted.
a. Corrupt; wicked; destitute of holiness or good principles.
depraver: A corrupted; he who citiates; a vilifier.
depravity: Corruption; a vitiated state; as the depravity of manners and morals.
A vitiated state of the heart; wickedness; corruption of moral principles; destitution of holiness or good principles.
deprecate: To pray against; to pray or intreat that a present evil may be removed, or an expected one averted. The judgments we would deprecate are not removed. We should all deprecate the return of war.

More generally, to regret; to have or to express deep sorrow at a present evil, or at one that may occur. This word is seldom used to express actual prayer; but it expresses deep regret that an evil exists or may exist, which implies a strong desire that it may be removed or averted.
deprecation: A praying against; a praying that an evil may be removed or prevented.
Intreaty; petitioning; an excusing; a begging pardon for.
deprecatory: That serves to deprecate; tending to remove or avert evil by prayer; as deprecatory letters.
deprecative: That serves to deprecate; tending to remove or avert evil by prayer; as deprecatory letters.
depreciate: To lessen the price of a thing; to cry down the price or value.
To undervalue; to represent as of little value or merit, or of less value than is commonly supposed; as, one author is apt to depreciate the works of another, or to depreciate their worth.
To lower the value. The issue of a superabundance of notes depreciates them, or depreciates their value.

To fall in value; to become of less worth. A paper currency will depreciate, unless it is convertible into specie. Estates are apt to depreciate in hte hands of tenants on short leases. Continental bills of credit, issued by the congress, during the revolution, depreciated to the one hundredth part of their nominal value.
depreciated: Lessened in value or price; undervalued.
depreciating: Lessening the price or worth; undervaluing.
depreciation: The act of lessening or crying down price or value.
The falling of value; reduction of worth; as the depreciation of bills of credit .
depredate: To plunder; to rob; to pillage; to take the property of an enemy or of a foreign country by force; as, the army depredated the enemys country. That kind of war which’ depredates and distresses individuals.
To prey upon; to waste; to spoil.
To devour; to destroy by eating; as, wild animals depredate the corn.
To take plunder or prey; to commit waste; as, the troops depredated on the country.
depredation: The act of plundering; a robbing; a pillaging.
Waste; consumption; a taking away by any act of violence. The sea often makes depredations on the land. Intemperance commits depredations on the constitution.
depredator: One who plunders, or pillages; a spoiler; a waster.
deprehend: To catch; to take unawares or by surprise; to seize, as a person committing an unlawful act.
To detect; to discover; to obtain the knowledge of.
deprehended: Taken by surprise; caught; seized; discovered.
deprehensible: That may be caught, or discovered.
deprehensibleness: Capableness of being caught or discovered.
deprehension: A catching or seizing; a discovery.
depress: To press down; to press to a lower state or position; as, to depress the end of a tube, or the muzzle of a gun.
To let fall; to bring down; as, to depress the eye.
To render dull or languid; to limit or diminish; as, to depress commerce.
To sink; to lower; to deject; to make sad; as, to depress the spirits or the mind.
depressing: Pressing down; lowering in place; letting fall; sinking; dejecting; abasing; impoverishing; rendering languid.
depression: The act of pressing down; or the state of being pressed down; a low state.
A hollow; a sinking or falling in of a surface; or a forcing inwards; as roughness consisting in little protuberances and depressions; the depression of the skull.
The act of humbling; abasement; as the depression of pride; the depression of the nobility.
A sinking of the spirits; dejection; a state of sadness; want of courage or animation; as depression of the mind.
A low state of strength; a state of body succeeding debility in the formation of disease.
depressive: Able or tending to depress or cast down.
depressor: He that presses down; an oppressor.
In anatomy, a muscle that depresses or draws down the part to which it is attached; as the depressor of the lower jaw, or of the eyeball. It is called also depriment or deprimens.
deprivable: That may be deprived. A chaplain shall be deprivable by the founder, not by the bishop.
deprivation: The act of depriving; a taking away.
A state of being deprived; loss; want; bereavement by loss of friends or of goods.

In law, the act of divesting a bishop or other clergyman of his spiritual promotion or dignity; the taking away of a preferment; deposition. This is of two kinds; a beneficio, and ab officio. The former is the deprivation of a minister of his living or preferment; the latter, of his order, and otherwise called deposition or degradation.
deprive: To take from; to bereave of something possessed or enjoyed; followed by of; as, to deprive a man of sight; to deprive one of strength, of reason, or of property. This has a general signification, applicable to a lawful or unlawful taking. God hath deprived her of wisdom. Job 39:17.
To hinder from possessing or enjoying; to debar. From his face I shall be hid, deprived of his blessed countenance.
To divest of an ecclesiastical preferment, dignity or office; to divest of orders; as a bishop, prebend or vicar.
deprived: Bereft; divested; hindered; stripped of office or dignity; deposed; degraded.
deprivement: The state of losing or being deprived.
depriver: He or that which deprives or bereaves.
depriving: Bereaving; taking away what is possessed; divesting; hindering from enjoying; deposing.
depth: Deepness; the distance or measure of a thing from the surface to the bottom, or to the extreme part downwards or inwards. The depth of a river may be ten feet. The depth of the ocean is unfathomable. The depth of a wound may be an inch. In a vertical direction, depth is opposed to highth.
The sea, the ocean. The depth closed me round about. Jonah 2:5.
The abyss; a gulf of infinite profundity. When he set a compass on the face of the depth. Proverbs 8:27.

The middle or highth of a season, as the depth of winter; or the middle, the darkest or stillest part, as the depth of night; or the inner part, a part remote from the border, as the depth of a wood or forest.
depurate: To purify; to free from impurities, heterogeneous matter or feculence; a chimical term.
depurated: Purified from heterogeneous matter, or from impurities.
depuration: The act of purifying or freeing fluids from heterogeneous matter. This is done by decantation, when the feculent matter is deposited on the bottom of the vessel; or by despumation, effected by boiling or fermentation, and skimming; or by filtration; or by fining or clarification.
The cleansing of a wound from impure matter.
depuratory: Cleansing; purifying; or tending to purify. A depuratory fever, is a fever that expels morbid matter by a free perspiration.
deputation: The act of appointing a substitute or representative to act for another; the act of appointing and sending a deputy or substitute to transact business for another, as his agent, either with a special commission and authority, or with general powers. This word may be used for the election of representatives to a legislature; but more generally it is employed to express the appointment of a special agent or commissioner, by an individual or public body, to transact a particular business.
A special commission or authority to act as the substitute of another; as, this man acts by deputation from the sheriff.
The person deputed; the person or persons authorized and sent to transact business for another; as, the General sent a deputation to the enemy to offer terms of peace.
depute: To appoint as a substitute or agent to act for another; to appoint and send with a special commission or authority to transact business in anothers name. The sheriff deputes a man to serve a writ. There is no man deputed by the king to hear. 2 Samuel! 15:3. The bishop may depute a priest to administer the sacrement.
deputed: Appointed as a substitute; appointed and sent with special authority to act for another.
deputing: Appointing as a substitute; appointed and sent with special authority to act for another.
deputize: To appoint a deputy; to empower to act for another, as a sheriff.
deputy: A person appointed or elected to act for another, especially a person sent with a special commission to act in the place of another; a lieutenant; a viceroy. A prince sends a deputy to a diet or council, to represent him and his dominions. A sheriff appoints a deputy to execute the duties of his office. The towns in New England send deputies to the legislature. In the latter sense, a deputy has general powers, and it is more common to use the word representative.
In law, one that exercises an office in anothers right, and the forfeiture or misdemeanor of such deputy shall cause the person he represents to lose his office.
deputy-collector: A person appointed to perform the duties of a collector of the customs, in place of the collector.
deputy-marshall: One appointed to act in the place of the marshal.
deputy-post-master: A person who is appointed to act as post-master, in subordination to the Post-Master General.
deputy-sheriff: A person deputed or authorized to perform the duties of the sheriff, as his substitute. In like manner, we use deputy-commissary, deputy-pay-master
der: prefixed to names of places, may be from Sax. deor, a wild beast, or from dur
deracinate: To pluck up by the roots; to extirpate.
deracinated: Plucked up by the roots; extirpated.
deracinating: Tearing up by the roots; extirpating.
deraign: or DERAINTo prove; to justify; to vindicate, as an assertion; to clear ones self.
deraignment: The act of deraining; proof; justification. A like word was formerly used in the sense of disordering, derangement, a discharge from a profession, or departure from a religious order.
derainment: The act of deraining; proof; justification. A like word was formerly used in the sense of disordering, derangement, a discharge from a profession, or departure from a religious order.
derange: To put out of order; to disturb the regular order of; to throw into confusion; as, to derange the plans of a commander, or the affairs of a nation. I had long supposed that nothing could derange or interrupt the course of putrefaction.
To embarrass; to disorder; as, his private affairs are deranged.
To disorder the intellect; to disturb the regular operations of reason.

To remove from place or office, as the personal staff of a principal military officer. Thus when a general officer resigns or is removed from office, the personal staff appointed by himself are said to be deranged.
deranged: Put out of order; disturbed; embarrassed; confused; disordered in mind; delirious; distracted.
derangement: A putting out of order; disturbance of regularity or regular course; embarrassment. ' .
Disorder of the intellect or reason; delirium; insanity; as a derangement of the mental organs.
deranging: Putting out of order; disturbing regularity or regular course; embarrassment; confusion.
derelict: In law, an article of goods, or any commodity, thrown away, relinquished or abandoned by the owner.
A tract of land left dry by the sea, and fit for cultivation or use.
dereliction: The act of leaving with an intention not to reclaim; an utter forsaking; abandonment.
The state of being left or abandoned.
A leaving or receding from; as the dereliction of the sea.
deride: To laugh at in contempt; to turn to ridicule or make sport of; to mock; to treat with scorn by laughter. The Pharisees also-derided him. Luke 16:14. Some, who adore Newton for his fluxions, deride him for his religion.
derided: Laughed at in contempt; mocked; ridiculed.
derider: One who laughs at another in contempt; a mocker; a scoffer.
deriding: Laughing at with contempt; mocking; ridiculing.
deridingly: By way of derision or mockery.
derision: The act of laughing at in contempt.
Contempt manifested by laughter; scorn. I am in derision daily. Jeremiah 20:7.
An object of derision or contempt; a laughing-stock. I was a derision to all my people. Lamentations 3:14.
derisive: Containing derision; mocking; ridiculing. Derisive taunts.
derivable: That may be derived; that may be drawn, or received, as from a source. Income is derivable from land, money or stocks.
That may be received from ancestors; as an estate derivable from an ancestor.
That may be drawn, as from premises; deducible; as an argument derivable from facts or preceding propositions.
That may be drawn from a radical word; as a word derivable from an Oriental root.
derivation: The act of deriving, drawing or receiving from a source; as the derivation of an estate from ancestors, or of profits from capital, or of truth or facts from antiquity.
In grammar, the drawing or tracing of a word from its root or original; as, derivation is from the L. Derivo, and the latter from rivus, a stream.
A drawing from, or turning aside from, a natural course or channel; as the derivation of water from its channel by lateral drains.
A drawing of humors from one part of the body to another; as the derivation of humors from the eye, by a blister on the neck.
derivative: Derived; taken or having proceeded from another or something preceding; secondary; as a derivative perfection; a derivative conveyance, as a release.
A derivative chord, in music, is one derived from a fundamental chord.

That which is derived; a word which takes its origin in another word, or is formed from it. Thus, depravity is a derivative from the L. Deravo, and acknowledge, from knowledge, and this from know, the primitive word.
In music, a chord not fundamental.
derivatively: In a derivative manner; by derivation.
derive: To draw from, as in a regular course or channel; to receive from a source by a regular conveyance. The heir derives an estate from his ancestors. We derive from Adam mortal bodies and natures prone to sin.
To draw or receive, as from a source or origin. We derive ideas from the senses, and instruction from good books.
To deduce or draw, as from a root, or primitive word. A hundred words are often derived from a single monosyllabic root, and sometimes a much greater number.
To turn from its natural course; to divert; as, to derive water from the main channel or current into lateral rivulets.
To communicate from one to another by descent. An excellent disposition is derived to your lordship from your parents.
derived: Drawn, as from a source; deduced; received; regularly conveyed; descended; communicated; transmitted.
deriver: One who derives, or draws from a source.
deriving: Drawing; receiving; deducing; communicating; diverting or turning into another channel.
dermal: Pertaining to skin; consisting of skin.
dermoid: Pertaining to the skin; a medical term.
dernier: Last; final; ultimate; as the dernier resort.
derogate: To repeal, annul or destroy the force and effect of some part of a law or established rule; to lessen the extent of a law; distinguished from abrogate. By several contrary customs, many of the civil and canon laws are controlled and derogated.
To lessen the worth of a person or thing; to disparage.
To take away; to detract; to lessen by taking away a part; as, say nothing to derogate from the merit or reputation of a brave man.
To act beneath ones rank, place or birth.
derogately: In a manner to lessen or take from.
derogating: Annulling a part. Lessening by taking from.
derogation: The act of annulling or revoking a law, or some part of it. More generally, the act of taking away or destroying the value or effect of any thing, or of limiting its extent, or of restraining its operation; as, an act of parliament is passed in derogation of the kings prerogative; we cannot do any thing in derogation of the moral law.

The act of taking something from merit, reputation or honor; a lessening of value or estimation; detraction; disparagement; with from or of; as, I say not this in derogation of Virgil; let nothing be said in derogation from his merit.
derogatory: Detracting or tending to lessen by taking something from; that lessens the extent, effect or value; with to. Let us entertain no opinions derogatory to the honor of God, or his moral government. Let us say nothing derogatory to the merit of our neighbor.

A derogatory clause in a testament, is a sentence or secret character inserted by the testator, of which he reserves the knowledge to himself, with a condition that no will be may make hereafter shall be valid, unless this clause is inserted word for word; a precaution to guard against later wills extorted by violence or obtained by suggestion.
dervis: A turkish priest or monk, who professes extreme poverty, and leads an austere life.
descant: A song or tune composed in parts.
Asong or tune with various modulations. The wakeful nightingale . All night long her amourous descant sung.
A discourse; discussion; disputation; animadversion, comment, or a series of comments.

The art of composing music in several parts. Descant is plain, figurative and double. Plain descant is the ground-work of musical compositions, consisting in the orderly disposition of concords, answering to simple counterpoint. Figurative or florid descant, is that part of an air in which some discords are concerned. Double descant, is when the parts are so contrived, that the treble may be made the base, and the base the treble.
To run a division or variety with the voice, on a musical ground in true measure; to sing.
descanting: Singing in parts or with various modulations; discoursing freely; commenting.
descend: To move or pass from a higher to a lower place; to move, come or go downwards; to fall; to sink; to run or flow down; applicable to any kind of motion or of body. We descend on the feet, on wheels, or by falling. A torrent descends from a mountain. The rains descended, and the floods came. Vatthew 7:25,
To go down, or to enter. He shall descend into battle and perish. 7 Samuel 26:10.
To come suddenly; to fall violently. And on the suitors let thy wrath descend.
To go in; to enter. He, with honest meditations fed, into himself descended.
To rush; to invade, as an enemy. The Grecian fleet descending on the town.
descendant: Any person proceeding from an ancestor in any degree; issue; offspring, in the line of generation, ad infinitum. We are all the descendants of Adam and Eve.
descendent: Proceeding from an original or ancestor.
descendibility: The quality of being descendible, or capable of being trnasmitted from ancestors; as the descendibility of an estate or of a crown.
descendible: That may be descended, or passed down; as, the hill is descendible.
That may descend from an ancestor to an heir; as a descendible estate.
descension: The act of going downwards; descent; a falling or sinking; declension; degradation.

In astronomy, right descension is an arch of the equinoctial, intercepted between the next equinoctial point and the intersection of the meridian, passing through the center of the object, at its setting, in a right sphere. Oblique descension, is an arch of the equinoctial, intercepted between the next equinoctial point and the horizon, passing through the center of the object, at its setting, in an oblique sphere. Oblique descension, is an arch of the equator which descends with the sun below the horizon of an oblique sphere. Descension of a sign, is an arch of the equator, which sets with such a sign or part of the zodiac, or any planet in it. Right descension of a sign, is an arch of the equator, which descends with the sign below the horizon of a right sphere; or the time the sign is setting in a right sphere.
descensive: Tending downwards; having power to descend.
descent: The act of descending; the act of passing from a higher to a lower place, by any form of motion, as by walking, riding, rolling, sliding, sinking or falling.
Inclination downward; obliquity; slope; declivity; as the descent of a hill, or a roof.
Progress downward; as the descent from higher to lower orders of beings.
Fall from a higher to a lower state or station.
A landing from ships; invasion of troops from the sea; as, to make a descent on Cuba.
describable: That may be described; capable of description.
describe: To delineate or mark the form or figure; as, to describe a circle by the compasses.
To make or exhibit a figure by motion; as, a star describes a circle or an ellipsis in the heavens.

To show or represent to others in words; to communicate the resemblance of a thing, by naming its nature, form or properties. The poet describes the Trojan horse. The historian describes the battle of Pharsalia. The moralist describes the effects of corrupt manners. The geographer describes countries and cities. V/ .
To represent by signs. A deaf and dumb man may describe a distant object. Our passions may be described by external motions.
To draw a plan; to represent by lines and other marks on paper, or other material; as, to describe the surface of the earth by a map or chart.
described: Represented in form by marks or figures; delineated; represented by words or signs.
describer: One who describes by marks, words or signs.
describing: Representing the form or figure of, by lines or marks; communicating a view of, by words or signs, or by naming the nature and properties.
descrier: One who espies, or discovers; a discoverer; a detecter.
description: The act of delineating, or representing the figure of any thing by a plan, to be presented to the eye.
The figure or appearance of any thing delineated, or represented by visible lines, marks, colors; as the description of a country, or of Solomons temple.

The act of representing a thing by words or by signs, or the passage containing such representation; a representation of names, nature of properties, that gives to another a view of the thing. Homer abounds with beautiful and striking descriptions.
A definition. All definitions must be less perfect descriptions of a material thing, than a visible figure or delineation.
The qualities expressed in a representation; as, a man of this description. Hence,
descriptive: Containing description; tending to describe; having the quality of representing; as a descriptive figure; a descriptive narration; a story descriptive of the age.
descry: To espy; to explore; to examine by observation. The house of Joseph sent to descry Bethel. Judges 1:23.
To detect; to find out; to discover any thing concealed.
To see; to behold; to have a sight of from a distance; as, the seamen descried land.
To give notice of something suddenly discovered.
desecrate: To divert from a sacred purpose or appropriation; opposed to consecrate; as, to desecrate a donation to a church.
To divest of a sacred character or office. The clergy-cannot suffer corporal punishment, without being previously desecrated.
desecrated: Diverted from a sacred purpose or appropriation; divested of a sacred character or office.
desecrating: Diverting from a purpose to which a thing is consecrated; divested of a sacred character or office.
desecration: The act of diverting from a sacred purpose or use to which a thing had been devoted; the act of diverting from a sacred character or office.
desert: Literally, forsaken; hence, uninhabited; as a desert isle. Hence, wild; untilled; waste; uncultivated; as a desert land or country.
Void; emprty; unoccupied. Full many a flower is born to blush unseen, and waste its sweetness on the desert air.

An uninhabited tract of land; a region in its natural state; a wilderness; a solitude; particularly, a vast sandy plain, as the deserts of Arabia and Africa. But the word may be applied to an uninhabited country covered with wood.
To forsake; to leave utterly; to abandon; to quit with a view not to return to; as, to desert a friend; to desert our country; to desert a cause.
deserter: A person who forsakes his cause, his post, or his party or friend; particularly, a soldier or seaman who quits the service without permission, and in violation of his engagement.
desertion: The act of forsaking or abandoning, as a party, a friend, a country, an army or military band, or a ship; the act of quitting, with an intention not to return.
The state of being forsaken by God; spiritual despondency. The agonies of a soul under desertion.
desertless: Without merit or claim to favor or reward.
deserve: To merit; to be worthy of; applied to good or evil.
To merit by labor or services; to have a just claim to an equivalent for good conferred. The laborer deserves his wages; he deserves the value of his services.
To merit by good actions or qualities in general; to be worthy of, on account of excellence. The virtuous man deserves esteem and commendation. A work of value deserves praise.
To be worthy of, in a bad sense; to merit by an evil act; as, to deserve blame or punishment. God exacteth of thee less than thine iniquity deserveth. Job 11:6.
To merit; to be worthy of or deserving; as, he deserves well or ill of his neighbor.
deservedly: Justly; according to desert, whether of good or evil. A man may be deservedly praised, blamed or punished.
deserver: He who deserves or merits; one who is worthy of; used generally in a good sense.
deserving: Meriting; having a just claim to reward; justly meriting punishment.
Worthy of reward or praise; meritorious; possessed of good qualities that entitle to approbation; as a deserving officer.
The act of meriting; desert; merit. If ye have done to him according to the deserving of his hands. Judges 9:16.
deshabille: or DESHABILAn undress; a loose morning dress; hence, any home dress; as, the lady is in deshabille.
desiccant: A medicine or application that dries a sore.
desiccate: to dry; to exhaust of moisture; to exhale or remove moisture from.
desiccation: The act of making dry; the state of being dried.
desiccative: Drying; tending to dry; that has the power to dry.
desideratum: Desiderata. That which is desired; that which is not possessed, but which is desirable; any perfection or improvement which is wanted. The longitude is a desideratum in navigation. A tribunal to settle national disputes without war is a great desideratum.
design: To delineate a form or figure by drawing the outline; to sketch; as in painting and other works of art.
To plan; to form an outline or representation of any thing. Hence,
To project; to form in idea, as a scheme. Hence,
To purpose or intend; as, a man designs to write an essay, or to study law.
designable: Capable of being designed or marked out.
designate: To mark out or show, so as to make known; to indicate by bisible lines, marks, description or something known and determinate; as, to designate the limits of a country; the limits are designated on the map; designate the spot where a star appears in the heavens; designate the place where our ancestors first landed.
To point out; to distinguish from others by indication; as, to be able to designate every individual who was concerned in a riot.

To appoint; to select or distinguish for a particular purpose; to assign; with for, as to designate an officer for the command of a station; or with to, as this captain was designated to that station.
designated: Marked out; indicated; shown; pointed out appointed.
designating: Marking out; indicating; pointing out; appointing.
designation: The act of pointing or marking out by signs or objects; as the designation of an estate by boundaries.
Indication; a showing or pointing; a distinguishing from others.
Appointment; direction; as, a claim to a throne grounded on the designation of a predecessor.
Appointment; a selecting and appointing; assignment; as the designation of an officer to a particular command.
Import; distinct application. Finite and infinite are primarily attributed in their first designation to things which have parts.
designator: A Roman officer who assigned to each person his rank and place in public shows and ceremonies.
designedly: By design; purposely; intentionally; opposed to accidentally, ignorantly, or inadvertently.
designer: One who designs, marks out or plans; one who frames a scheme or project; a contriver.
One who plots; one who lays a scheme; in an ill sense.
designing: Forming a design; pinning; delineating the outline; drawing figures on a plane.
a. In an ill sense, artful; insidious; intriguing; contriving schemes of mischief; henceDesigning men are always liable to suspicion.
desirable: Worthy of desire; that is to be wished for with sincerity or earnestness. An easy address is a desirable accomplishment; real virtue is still more desirable.
Pleasing; agreeable. All of them desirable young men. Ezekiel 23:12, 23.
desire: An emotion or excitement of the mind, directed to the attainment or possession of an object from which pleasure, sensual, intellectual or spiritual, is expected; a passion excited by the love of an object, or uneasiness at the want of it, and directed to its attainment or possession. Desire is a wish to possess some gratification or source of happiness which is supposed to be obtainable. A wish may exist for something that is or is not abtainable. Desire, when directed solely to sensual enjoyment, differs little from appetite. In other languages, desire is expressed by longing or reaching toward, and when it is ardent or intense, it approaches to longing, but the word in English usually expresses less than longing. We endeavored--to see your face with great desire. Thessalonians 2:17. Thou satisfiest the desires of every living thing. Psalm 145:16. Desire is that internal act, which, by
A prayer or request to obtain: . He will fulfill the desire of them that fear him. Psalm 145:179.
The object of desire; that which is desired. The desire of all nations shall come. Haggai 2:7.
Love; affection. His desire is toward me. Song of Solomon 7:10.
Appetite; lust. Fulfilling the desires of the flesh. Ephesians 2:3.
desirer: One who desires or asks; one who wishes.
desiring: Wishing for; coveting; asking; expressing a wish; soliciting.
desirous: Wishing for; wishing to obtain; coveting; solicitous to possess and enjoy. Be not desirous of his dainties. Proverbs 23:3. Jesus knew they were desirous to ask him. John 16:19.
desirousness: The state or affection of being desirous.
desist: To stop; to cease to act or proceed; to forbear; with from; as, he desisted from his purpose; let us desist.
desistance: A ceasing to act or proceed; a stopping.
desk: An inclining table for the use of writers and readers; usually made with a box or drawer underneath, and sometimes with a book-case above.
The pulpit in a church, and figuratively the clerical profession. The man appears well in the desk. He intends one son for the bar, and another for the desk.
To shut up in a desk; to treasure.
desmine: A mineral that crystalizes in little silken tufts, which accompany spinellane in the lava of extinct volcanoes on the banks of the Rhine.
desolate: Destitute or deprived of inhabitants; desert; uninhabited; denoting either stripped of inhabitants, or never having been inhabitated; as a desolate isle; a desolate wilderness. I will make the cities of Judah desolate, without an inhabitant. Jeremiah 9:11.
Laid waste; in a ruinous condition; neglected; destroyed; as desolate altars; desolate towers. Ezek. Zeph.
Solitary; without a companion; afflicted. Tamar remained desolate in Absaloms house. 2 Samuel 13:20.
Deserted of God; deprived of comfort. My heart within me is desolate. Psalm 143:4.&
To deprive of inhabitants; to make desert. The earth was nearly desolated by the flood.
desolater: One who lays waste or desolates; that which desolates.
desolation: The act of desolating destruction or expulsion of inhabitants; destruction; ruin; waste. Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation. Matthew 12:25.
A place deprived of inhabitants, or otherwise wasted, ravaged and ruined. How is Babylon become a desolation among the nations. Jeremiah 50:23.
A desolate state; gloominess; sadness; destitution. The abomination of desolation, Roman armies which ravaged and destroyed Jerusalem. Matthew 24:15.
despair: Hopelessness; a hopeless state; a destitution of hope or expectation. We are perplexed, but not in despair. 2 Corinthians 4:8. All safety in despair of safety placed.
That which causes despair; that of which there is no hope. The mere despair of surgery, he cures.
Loss of hope in the mercy of God.
To be without hope; to hive up all hope or expectation; followed by of. We despaired even of life. 2 Corinthians 1:8. Never despair of Gods blessings here, or of his reward hereafter.
despairing: Giving up all hope or expectation.
despairingly: In a despairing manner; in a manner indicating hopelessness; as, he speaks despairingly of the sick man’s recovery.
desperado: A desperate fellow; a furious man; a madman; a person urged by furious passions; one fearless, or regardless of safety.
desperate: Without hope. I am desperate of obtaining her.
Without care of safety; rash; fearless of danger; as a desperate man. Hence,
Furious, as a man in despair.
Hopeless; despaired of; lost beyond hope of recovery; irretrievable; irrecoverable; forlorn. We speak of a desperate case of disease, desperate fortunes, a desperate situation or condition.
In a popular sense, great in the extreme; as a desperate sot or fool .
desperately: In a desperate manner; as in despair; hence, furiously; with rage; madly; without regard to danger or safety; as, the troops fought desperately.
In a popular sense, greatly; extremely; violently. She fell desperately in love with him.
desperation: A despairing; a giving up of hope; as desperation of success.
Hopelessness; despair; as, the men were in a state of desperation. Hence,
Fury; rage; disregard of safety or danger; as, the men fought with desperation; they were urged’ to desperation.
despicable: That may be or deserves to be despised; contemptible; mean; vile; worthless; applicable equally to persons and things; as a despicable man; despicable company; a despicable gift.
despicableness: The quality or state of being despicable; meanness; vileness; worthlessness.
despicably: Meanly; vilely; contemptibly; as despicably poor.
despise: To contemn; to scorn; to disdain; to have the lowest opinion of. Fools despise wisdom and instruction. Proverbs 1:7. Else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Watihew 6:24.
despite: Extreme malice; violent hatred; malignity; malice irritated or enraged; active malignity; angry hatred. With all thy despite against the land of israel. Ezekiel 25:6.
Defiance with contempt, or contempt of opposition. He will rise to fame in despite of his enemies.
An act of malice or contempt; as a despite to the Most High.
To vex; to offend; to tease.
despiteful: Full of spite; malicious; malignant; as a despiteful enemy. Hater of God, despiteful, proudRomans 1:30.
despitefully: With despite; maliciously; contemptuously. Pray for them that despitefully use you. Vatthew 5:44.
despoil: To strip; to take from by force; to rob; to deprive; followed by of; as, to despoil one of arms; to despoil of honors; to despoil of innocence.
To strip or divest by any means.
despoiler: One who strips by force; a plunderer.
despoliation: The act of despoiling; a stripping.
despond: To be cast down; to be depressed or dejected in mind; to fail in spirits. I should despair, or at least despond.

To lose all courage, spirit or resolution; to sink by loss of hope. Others depress their own mind, and despond at the first difficulty: . Note. The distinction between despair and despond is well marked in the foregoing passage from Scott. But although despair implies a total loss of hope, which despond does not, at least in every case, yet despondency is followed by the abandonment of effort, or cessation of action, and despair sometimes impelss to violent action, even to rage.
despondency: A sinking or dejection of spirits at the loss of hope; loss of courage at the failure of hope, or in deep affliction, or at the prospect of insurmountable difficulties.
despondent: Losing courage at the loss of hope; sinking into dejection; depressed and inactive in despair.
desponding: Losing courage to act, in consequence of loss of hope, or of deep calamity, or of difficulties deemed insurmountable; sinking into dejection; despairing, with depression of spirits; despairingly.
despondingly: In a desponding manner; with dejection of spirits; despairingly.
despot: An emperor, king or price invested with absolute power, or ruling without any control from men, constitution or laws. Hence in a general sense, a tyrant.
despotic: Absolute in power; independent of control from men, constitution or laws; arbitrary in the exercise of power; as a despotic prince.
Unlimited or unrestrained by constitution, laws or men; absolute; arbitrary; as despotic authority or power.
despotical: Absolute in power; independent of control from men, constitution or laws; arbitrary in the exercise of power; as a despotic prince.
Unlimited or unrestrained by constitution, laws or men; absolute; arbitrary; as despotic authority or power.
despotically: With unlimited power; arbitrarily; in a despotic manner.
despotism: Absolute power; authority unlimited and uncontrolled by men, constitution or laws, and depending alone on the will of the prince; as the despotism of a Turkish sultan.
An arbitrary government, as that of Turkey and Persia.
despumate: To foam; to froth; to form froth or scum.
despumation: The act of throwing off excrementitious matter and forming a froth or scum on the surface of liquor; clarification; scumming.
desquamation: A scaling or exfoliation of bone; the separation of the cuticle in small scales.
dessert: A service of fruits and sweetmeats, at the close of an entertainment; the last course at the table, after the meat is removed.
destination: The act of destining, or appointing.
The purpose for which any thing is intended or appointed; end or ultimate design. Every animal is fitted for its destination.
The place to which a thing is appointed, as the ship left her destination; but it is more usual to say, the place of her destination.
destine: To set, ordain or appoint to a use, purpose, state or place. We destine a son to the ministerial office; a house for a place of worship; a ship for the London trade or to Lisbon; and we are all destined to a future state of happiness or misery.
To fix unalterable, as by a divine decree; as the destined hour of death.
To doom; to devote; to appoint unalterably.
destined: Ordained; appointed by previous determination; devoted; fixed unalterably.
destiny: State or condition appointed or predetermined; ultimate fate; as, men are solicitous to know their future destiny, which is however happily concealed from them.

Invincible necessity; fate; a necessity or fixed order of things established by a divine decree, or by an indissoluble connection of causes and effects. But who can turn the stream of destiny? Destinies, the fates, or supposed powers which preside over himan life, spin it out, and determine it; called by the Latins.
destitute: Not having or possessing; wanting; as destitute of virtue, or of peity; destitute of food and clothing. It differs from deprived, as it does not necessarily imply previous possession.
Needy; abject; comfortless; friendless. He will regard the prayer of the destitute. Psalm 102:17.
One who is without friends or comfort.
destitution: Want; absence of a thing; a state in which something is wanted or not possessed; poverty.
destroy: To demolish; to pull down; to separate the parts of an edifice, the union of which is necessary to constitute the thing; as, to destroy a house or temple; to destroy a fortification.
To ruin; to annihilate a thing by demolishing or by burning; as, to destroy a city: .
To ruin; to bring to naught; to annihilate; as, to destroy a theory or scheme; to destroy a government; to destroy influence.
To lay waste; to make desolate. Go up against this land, and destroy it.Isaiah 36:10.
To kill; to slay; to extirpate; applied to men or other animals. Ye shall destroy all this people. Numbers 32:15. All the wicked will he destroy. Psalm 145:20.
destroyable: That may be destroyed. Plants scarcely destroyable by the weather.
destroyed: Demolished; pulled down; ruined; annihilated; devoured; swept away; etc.
destroyer: One who destroys, or lays waste; one who kills a man, or an animal, or who ruins a country, cities
destroying: Demolishing; laying waste; killing; annihilating; putting an end to.
destructibility: The quality of being capable of destruction.
destructible: Liable to destruction; capable of being destroyed.
destruction: The act of destroying; demolition; a pulling down; subversion; ruin, by whatever means; as the destruction of buildings, or of towns. Destruction consists in the annihilation of the form of any theing; that form of parts which constitues it what it is; as the destruction of grass or herbage by eating; of a forest, by cutting down the trees; or it denotes a total annihilation; as the destruction of a particular government; the destruction of happiness.
Death; murder; slaughter; massacre. There was a deadly destruction throughout all the city. 7 Samuel 5:11.
Ruin, Destruction and misery are in their ways. Romans 3:16.
Eternal death. Broad is the way that leadeth to destruction. Matthew 7:13.
Cause of destruction; a consuming plague; a destroyer. The destruction that wasteth at noon-day. Psalm 97:6.
destructive: Causing destruction; having the quality of destroying; ruinous; mischievous; pernicious; with of or to; as a destructive fire or famine. Intemperance is destructive of health; evil examples are destructive to the morals of youth.
destructively: With destruction; ruinously; mischievously; with power to destroy; as destructively lewd or intemperate.
destructiveness: The quality of destroying or ruining.
desudation: A sweating; a profuse or morbid sweating, succeeded by an eruption of pustles, called heat-pimples.
desuetude: The cessation of use; disuse; discontinuance of practice, custom or fashion. Habit is contracted by practice, and lost by desuetude. Words in every language are lost by desuetude.
desulphuration: The act or operation of depriving of sulphur.
desultorily: In a desultory manner; without method; loosely.
desultoriness: A desultory manner; unconnectedness; a passing from one thing to another without order or method.
desultory: Leaping; passing from one thing or subject to another, without order or natural connection; unconnected; immethodical; as a desultory conversation.
Coming suddenly; started at the moment; not proceeding from natural order or connection with what precedes; as a desultory thought.
detach: To separate or disunite; to disengage; to part from; as, to detach the coats of a bulbous root from each other; to detach a man from the interest of the minister, or from a party.
To separate men from their companies or regiments; to draw from companies or regiments, as a party of men, and send them on a particular service.
To select ships from a fleet and send them on a separate service.
detached: Separated; parted from; disunited; drawn and sent on a separate service. 2 .a. Separate; as detached parcels or portions.
detaching: Separating; parting from; drawing and sending on a separate employment.
detachment: A body of troops, selected or taken from the main army, and employed on some special service or expedition.
A number of ships, taken from a fleet, and sent on a separate service.
detail: To relate, report or narrate in particulars; to recite the particulars of; to particularize; to relate minutely and distinctly; as, he detailed all the facts in due order.
To select, as an officer or soldier from a division, brigade, regiment or battalion.
A narration or report of particulars; a minute and particular account. He related the story in detail. He gave a detail of all the transactions.
A selecting of officer or soldiers from the rosters.
detailed: Related in particulars; minutely recited; selected.
detain: To keep back or from; to withhold; to keep what belongs to another. Detain not the wages of the hireling.
To keep or restrain from proceeding, either going or coming; to stay or stop. We were detained by the rain. Let us detain thee, till we have made ready a kid. Judges 13:15.
detained: Withheld; kept back; prevented from going or coming; held; restrained.
detainer: One who withholds what belongs to another; one who detains, stops or prevents from going.
In law, a holding or keeping possession of what belongs to another; detention of what is anothers, though the original taking may be lawful.
detaining: Withholding what belongs to another; holding back; restraining from going or coming; holding in custody.
detect: Literally, to uncover; hence, to discover; to find out; to bring to light; as, to detect the ramifications and inosculations of the fine vessels. But this word is especially applied to the discovery of secret crimes and artifices. We detect a thief, or the crime of stealing. We detect the artifices of the man, or the man himself. We detect what is concealed, especially what is concealed by design.
detected: Discovered; found out; laid open; brought to light.
detecter: A discoverer; one who finds out what another attempts to conceal.
detection: The act of detecting; discovery of a person or thing attempted to be concealed; as the detection of a thief or burglarian; the detection of fraud or forgery; the detection of artifice, device or a plot.
Discovery of any thing before hidden, or unknown. The sea and rivers are instrumental to the detection of amber and other fossils, by washing away the earth that concealed them.
detent: A stop in a clock, which by being lifted up or let down, locks and unlocks the clock in striking.
detention: The act of detaining; a withholding from another his right; a keeping what belongs to another, and ought to be restored.
Confinement; restraint; as detention in custody.
Delay from necessity; a detaining; as the detention of the mail by bad roads.
deter: To discourage and stop by fear; to stop or prevent from acting or proceeding, by danger, difficulty or other consideration which disheartens, or countervails the motive for an act. We are often deterred from out duty by trivial difficulties. The state of the road or a cloudy sky may deter a man from undertaking a journey. A million of frustrated hopes will not deter us from new experiments.
To prevent by prohibition or danger.
deterge: deterj. To cleanse; to purge away foul or offending matter, from the body, or from an ulcer.
detergent: A medicine that has the power of cleansing the vessels or skin from offending matter.
deterging: Cleansing; carrying off obstructions or foul matter.
deteriorate: To grow worse; to be impaired in quality to degenerate; opposed to meliorate.
To make worse; to reduce in quality; as, to deteriorate a race of men of their condition.
deteriorating: Becoming worse or inferior in quality.
deterioration: A growing or making worse; the state of growing worse.
deteriority: Worse sate or quality; as deteriority of diet.
determent: The act of deterring; the cause of deterring; that which deters.
determinable: That may be decided with certainty.
That may end or be determined.
determinate: Limited; fixed; definite; as a determinate quantity of matter.
Established; settled; positive; as a determinate rule or order. The determinate counsel of God.Acts 2:23.
Decisive; conclusive; as a determinate resolution or judgment.
determinately: With certainty. The principles of religion are determinately true or false.
determinateness: The state of being determinate, certain, or precise.
determination: The act of determining or deciding.
Decision of a question in the mind; firm resolution; settled purpose; as, they have acquainted me with their determination.
Judicial decision; the ending of a controversy or suit by the judgment of a court. Justice is promoted by a speedy determination of causes, civil and criminal.
Absolute direction to a certain end. Remissness can by no means consist with a constant determination of the will to the greatest apparent good.
An ending; a putting an end to; as the determination of a will.
determinative: That uncontrollably directs to a certain end. The determinative power of a just cause.
Limiting; that limits or bounds; as, a word may be determinative and limit the subject.
determine: To end; particularly, to end by the decision or conclusion of a cause, or of a doubtful or controverted point; applicable to the decisions of the mind, or to judicial decisions. We say, I had determined this question in my own mind; the court has determined the cause.
To end and fix; to settle ultimately; as, this event determined his fate.
To fix on; to settle or establish; as, to determine the proper season for planting seeds. God--hath determined the times before appointed. Acts 17:26.
To end; to limit; to bound; to confine. Yonder hill determines our view. Knowledge is determined by the sight.

To give a direction to; to influence the choice; that is, to limit to a particular purpose or direction; as, this circumstance determined him to the study of law. Also, to give a direction to material bodies in their course; as, impulse may determine a moving body to this or that point.
determined: Ended; concluded; decided; limited; fixed; settled; resolved; directed. 2.a. Having a firm or fixed purpose, as a determined man; or manifesting a firm resolution, as a determined countenance.
determining: Ending; deciding; fixing; settling; resolving; limiting; directing.
deterration: The uncovering of any thing which is buried or covered with earth; a taking from out of the earth.
deterred: Discouraged or prevented from proceeding or acting, by fear, difficulty or danger.
deterring: Discouraging or influencing not to proceed or act, by fear, difficulty, danger, or prospect of evil.
detersion: The act of cleansing, as a sore.
detersive: Cleansing; having power to cleanse from offending matter.
A medicine which has the power of cleansing ulcers, or carrying off foul matter.
detest: To abhor; to abominate; to hate extremely; as, to detest crimes or meanness.
detestable: Extremely hateful; abominable; very odious; deserving abhorrence. Thou hast defiled my sanctuary with all thy detestable things. Ezekiel 5:11.
detestation: Extreme hatred; abhorrence; with of. The good man entertains uniformly a detestation of sin.
dethrone: To remove or drive from a throne; to depose; to divest of royal authority and dignity.
To divest of rule or power, or of supreme power. The protector was dethroned.
dethronement: Removal from a throne; deposition of a king, emperor or prince.
dethroning: Driving from a throne; depriving of regal power.
detinue: In law, a writ of detinue is one that lies against him who wrongfully detains goods or chattels delivered to him, or in his possession. This writ lies for a thing certain and valuable, as for a horse, cow, sheep, plate, cloth, to recover the thing itself or damages for the detainer.
detonate: In chemistry, to cause to explode; to burn or inflame with a sudden report.
To explode; to burn with a sudden report. Niter detonates with sulphur.
detonating: Exploding; inflaming with a sudden report.
detonation: An explosion or sudden report made by the inflammation of certain combustible bodies, as fulminating gold. Detonation is not decrepitation.
detonization: The acct of exploding, as certain combustible bodies.
detonize: To cause to explode; to burn with an explosion; to calcine with detonation.
To explode; to burn with a sudden report. This precipitate-detonizes with a considerable noise.
detort: To twist; to wrest; to pervert; to turn from the original or plain meaning.
detract: Literally, to draw from. Hence, to take away from reputation or merit, through envy, malice or other motive; hence, to detract from, is to lessen or depreciate reputation or worth; to derogate from. Never circulate reports that detract from the reputation or honor of your neighbor, without obvious necessity to justify the act.
To take away; to withdraw, in a literal sense.
detraction: The act of taking something from the reputation or worth of another, with the view to lessen him in estimation; censure; a lessening of worth; the act of depreciating another, from envy or malice. Detraction may consist in representing merit, as less than it really is; or in the imputation of faults, vices or crimes, which impair reputation; and if such imputation is false, it is slander or defamation.
detractive: Having the quality or tendency to lessen the worth or estimation.
detractor: One who takes away or impairs the reputation of another injuriously; one who attempts to lessen the worth or honor of another.
detractory: Derogatory; defamatory by denial of desert; with from.
detractress: A female detractor; a censorious woman.
detriment: Loss; damage; injury; mischief; harm; diminution. We speak of detriment to interest, property, religion, morals, reputation, and to land or buildings. It is a word of very general application.
detrimental: Injurious; hurtful; causing loss or damage. A spirit of speculation may be detrimental to regular commerce.
detritus: In geology, a mass of substances worn off or detached from solid bodies by attrition; as diluvial detritus.
detrude: To thrust down; to push down with force.
detruncate: To cut off; to lop; to shorten by cutting.
detrusion: s as z. The act of thrusting or driving down.
deuce: Two; a card with two spots; a die with two spots; a term used in gaming.
deuterogamist: One who marries the second time.
deuterogamy: A second marriage after the death of the first husband or wife.
deuteronomy: The second law, or second giving of the law by Moses; the name given to the fifth book of the Pentateuch.
deutoxyd: In chemistry, a substance oxydized in the second degree.
devaporation: The change of vapor into water, as in the generation of rain.
devastate: To lay waste; to waste; to ravage; to desolate; to destroy improvements.
devastation: Waste; ravage; desolation; destruction of works of art and natural productions which are necessary or useful to man; havock; as by armies, fire, flood.
IN law, waste of the goods of the deceased by an executor, or administrator.
develop: To uncover; to unfold; to lay open; to disclose or make known something concealed or withheld from notice. The General began to develop the plan of his operations. These serve to develop its tenets.
To unravel; to unfold what is intricate; as, to develop a plot.
development: An unfolding; the discovering of something secret or withheld from the knowledge of others; disclosure; full exhibition.
devest: To strip; to deprive of clothing or arms; to take off.
To deprive; to take away; as, to devest a man or nation of rights.
In law, to alienate, as title or right.
In law, to be lost or alienated, as a title or an estate.
devested: Stripped of clothes; deprived; freed from; alienated or lost, as title.
devesting: Stripping of clothes; depriving; freeing from; alienating.
devexity: A bending downward; a sloping; incurvation downward.
deviate: To turn aside or wander from the common or right way, course or line, either in a literal or figurative sense; as, to deviate from the common track or path, or from a true course. There nature deviates, and here wanders will.
To stray from the path of duty; to wander, in a moral sense; to err; to sin.
deviation: A wandering or turning aside from the right way, course or line.
Variation from a common or established rule, or from analogy.
A wandering from the path of duty; want of conformity to the rules prescribed by God; error; sin; obliquity of conduct.
In commerce, the voluntary departure of a ship, without necessity, from the regular and usual course of the specific voyage insured. This discharges the underwriters from their responsibility.
device: That which is formed by design, or invented; scheme; artificial contrivance; stratagem; project; sometimes in a good sense; more generally in a bad sense, as artifices are usually employed for bad purposes. In a good sense: . His device is against Babylon, to destroy it. Jeremiah 51:117. In a bad sense: . He disappointeth the devices of the crafty. Job 5:12. They imagined a mischievous device. Psalm 21:17.

An emblem intended to represent a family, person, action or quality, with a suitable motto; used in painting, sculpture and heraldry. It consists in a metaphorical similitude between the things representing and represented, as the figure of a plow representing agriculture. Knights-errant used to distinguish themselves by devices on their shields.
Invention; genius; faculty of devising; as a man of noble device.
devil: In the Christian theology, an evil spirit or being; a fallen angel, expelled from heaven for rebellion against God; the chief of the apostate angels; the implacable enemy and tempter of the human race. In the New Testament, the word is frequently and erroneously used for demon.
A very wicked person, and in ludicrous language, an great evil. In profane language, it is an expletive expressing wonder, vexation.
An idol, or false god. Leviticus 17:7; 2 Chronicles 11:15.
devilish: Partaking of the qualities of the devil; diabolical; very evil and mischievous; malicious; as a devilish scheme; devilish wickedness.
Having communication with the devil; pertaining to the devil.
Excessive; enormous; in a vulgar and ludicrous sense; as a devilish cheat.
devilishly: In a manner suiting the devil; diabolically; wickedly.
Greatly; excessively; in a vulgar sense.
devious: Out of the common way or track; as a devious course.
Wandering; roving; rambling. To bless the wildly devious morning walk.
Erring; going astray from rectitude or the divine precepts. One devious step at first may lead into a course of habitual vice.
devisable: That may be bequeathed or given by will.
That can be invented or contrived.
devise: To invent; to contrive; to form in the mind by new combinations of ideas, new applications of principles, or new arrangement of parts; to excogitate; to strike out by thought; to plan; to scheme; to project; as, to devise an engine or machine; to devise a new mode of writing; to devise a plan of defense; to devise arguments. To devise curious works in gold and silver. Exodus 35:32. In a bad sense: . Devise not evil against thy neighbor. Proverbs 3:29.
To give or bequeath by will, as land or other real estate.
To consider; to contrive; to lay a plan; to form a scheme. Devise how you will use him, when he comes. Formerly followed by of; as, let us devise of ease.
Primarily, a dividing or division; hence, the act of bequeathing by will; the act of giving or distributing real estate by a testator.
devisee: The person to whom a devise is made; one to whom real estate is bequeathed.
deviser: One who contrives or invents; a contriver; an inventor.
devising: Contriving; inventing; forming a scheme or plan.
devisor: One who gives by will; one who bequeaths lands or tenements.
devoid: Void; empty; vacant; applied to place.
Destitute; not possessing; as devoid of understanding.
Free from; as devoid of fear or shame.
devoir: Primarily, service or duty. Hence, an act of civility or respect; respectful notice due to another; as, we paid our devoirs to the queen, or to the ladies.
devolution: The act of rolling down; as the devolution of earth into a valley.
Removal from one person to another; a passing or falling upon a successor.
devolve: To roll down; to pour or flow with windings. Through splendid kingdoms he devolves his maze.
To move from one person to another; to deliver over, or from one possessor to a successor. The king devolved the care and disposition of affairs on the duke or Ormond.

devolv. Literally, to roll down; hence, to pass from one to another; to fall by succession from one possessor to his successor. In the absence of the commander in chief, the command devolved on the next officer in rank. On the death of the prince, the crown devolved on his eldest son.
devolved: Rolled down; passed over to another.
devolving: Rolling down; falling to a successor.
devote: To appropriate by vow; to set apart ro dedicate by a solemn act; to consecrate. No devoted thing that a man shall devote to the Lord--shall be sold or redeemed. Every thing devoted thing is most holy to the Lord. Leviticus 27:28.
To give up wholly; to addict; to direct the attention wholly or chiefly; to attach; as, to devote ones self to science; to devote ourselves to our friends, or to their interest or pleasure.
To give up; to resign; as, aliens were devoted to rapine; the city was devoted to the flames.
To doom; to consign over; as, to devote one to destruction.
To execrate; to doom to evil.
devoted: Appropriated by vow; solemnly set apart or dedicated; consecrated; addicted; given up; doomed; consigned.
devotedness: The state of being devoted or given; addictedness; as devotedness to religion.
devotee: One who is wholly devoted; particularly, one given wholly to religion; one who is superstitiously given to religious duties and ceremonies; a bigot.
devoter: One that devotes; also, a worshiper.
devoting: Giving or appropriating by vow; solemnly setting apart or dedicating; consecrating; giving wholly; addicting; dooming; consigning.
devotion: The state of being dedicated, consecrated, or solemnly set apart for a particular purpose.

A solemn attention to the Supreme Being in worship; a yielding of the heart and affections to God, with reverence, faith and piety, in religious duties, particularly in prayer and meditation; devoutness.
External worship; acts of religion; performance of religious duties. As I passed by and beheld your devotions. Acts 17:23.
Prayer to the Supreme Being. A Christian will be regular in his morning and evening devotions.
An act of reverence, respect or ceremony.
devotional: Pertaining to devotion; used in devotion; as a devotional posture; devotional exercises.
Suited to devotion; as a devotional frame of mind.
devotionalist: A person given to devotion; or one superstitiously or formally devout.
devotionist: A person given to devotion; or one superstitiously or formally devout.
devour: To eat up; to eat with greediness; to eat ravenously, as a beast of prey, or as a hungry man. We will say, some evil beast hath devoured him. Genesis 37:20. In the morning, he shall devour the prey. Genesis 49:27.

To destroy; to consume with rapidity and violence. I will send a fire into the house of Hazael, which shall devour the palaces of Ben-Hadad. Amos 1:4. Famine and pestilence shall devour him. Ezekiel 7:75.
To destroy; to annihilate; to consume. He seemed in swiftness to devour the way.
To waste; to consume; to spend in dissipation and riot. As soon as this thy son had come, who hath devoured thy living with harlots. Luke 15:30.
To consume wealth and substance by fraud, oppression, or illegal exactions. Ye devour widows houses. IVatithew 23:14.
devoured: Eaten; swallowed with greediness; consumed; destroyed; wasted; slain.
devourer: One who devours; he or that which eats, consumes or destroys; he that preys on.
devouring: Eating greedily; consuming; wasting; destroying; annihilating.
devout: Yielding a solemn and reverential attention to God in religious exercises, particularly in prayer. We must be constant and devout in the worship of God.
Pious; devoted to religion; religious. Simeon was a just man and devout. Luke 2:25. Devout men carried Stephen to his burial. Acts 8:2.
Expressing devotion or piety; as, with eyes devout.
Sincere; solemn; earnest; as, you have my devout wishes for your safety.
devoutly: With solemn attention and reverence to God; with ardent devotion. He was devoutly engaged in prayer.
Piously; religiously; with pious thoughts; as, he viewed the cross devoutly.
Sincerely; solemnly; earnestly; as, a consummation devoutly to be wished.
devoutness: The quality or state of being devout.
dew: The water or moisture collected or deposited on or near the surface of the earth, during the night, by the escape of the heat which held the water in solution.
To wet with dew; to moisten.
dew-berry: The fruit of a species of brier or bramble, that creeps along the ground, of the genus Rubus.
dew-drop: A drop of dew, which sparkles at sunrise; a spangle of dew.
dew-lap: The flesh that hangs from the throat of oxen, which laps or licks the dew in grazing.
In Shakespeare, a lip flaccid with age.
dew-worm: A worm, called otherwise earth-worm, a species of Lumbricus, which lives just under the surface of the ground. It is of a pale red color, and does no injury to plants.
dewy: Partaking of dew; like dew; as dewy mist.
Moist with dew; as dewy fields. His dewy locks distilled Ambrosia.
dexter: Right, as opposed to left; a term used in heraldry, to denote the right side of a shield or coat of arms; as, bend-dexter; dexter-point.
