Power to direct, control, use and dispose of at pleasure; right of possession and use without being accountable; as the private dominion of individuals.
Territory under a government; region; country; district governed, or within the limits of the authority of a prince or state; as the British dominions.
Government; right of governing. Jamaica is under the dominion of Great Britain.
domino: A kind of hood; a long dress; a masquerade dress.
domite: A mineral named from Dome in Auvergne, in France, of a white or grayish white color, having the aspect and gritty feel of a sandy chalk. DON. A title in Spain, formerly given to noblemen and gentlemen only, but now common to all classes. It is commonly supposed to be contracted from dominus, dom, and the Portuguese dono, the master or owner of any thing, gives some countenance to the opinion. It coincides nearly with Heb.: judge, ruler or lord. It was formerly used in England, and writter by Chaucer Dan. Dona, or duena, the feminine of don, is the title of a lady, in Spain and Portugal. DON.
don: To put on; to invest with.
donacite: A petrified shell of the genus Donax.
donary: A thing given to a sacred use.
donation: The act of giving or bestowing; a grant. That right we hold by his donation.

In law, the act or contract by which a thing or the use of it is transferred to a person, or corporation, as a free gift. To be valid, a donation supposes capacity both in the donor to give and donee to take, and requires consent, acceptance and delivery.

That which is given or bestowed; that which is transferred to another gratuitously, or without a valuable consideration; a gift; a grant. Donation is usually applied to things of more value than present. Mr. Boudinot made a donation of ten thousand dollars to the American Bible Society.
donatist: One of the sect founded by Donatus. They held that theirs was the only pure church, and that baptism and ordination, unless by their church, were invalid.
donative: A gift] a largess; a gratuity; a present; a dole. The Romans were entertained with shows and donatives.
In the canon law, a benefice given and collated to a person, by the founder or patron, without either presentation, institution or induction by the ordinary.
Vested or vesting by donation; as a donative advowson.
done: A word by which agreement to a proposal is expressed; as in laying a wager, an offer being made, the person accepting or agreeing says, done; that is, it is agreed, I agree, I accept.
donee: The person to whom a gift or donation is made.
The person to whom lands or tenements are given or granted; as a donee in fee-simple or fee-tail.
donor: One who gives or bestows; one who confers any thing gratuitously; a benefactor.
One who grants an estate; as, a conditional fee may revert to the donor, if the donee has no heirs of his body.
donship: The quality or rank of a gentleman or knight.
doom: To judge. Thou didst not doom so strictly.
To condemn to any punishment; to consign by a decree or sentence; as, the criminal is doomed to chains.
To pronounce sentence or judgment on. Absolves the just, and dooms the guilty souls.
To command authoritatively. Have I a tongue to doom my brothers death.
To destine; to fix irrevocably the fate or direction of; as, we are doomed to suffer for our sins and errors.
doomage: A penalty or fine for neglect.
doomsday: The day of the final judgment; the great day when all men are to be judged and consigned to endless happiness or misery.
The day of sentence or condemnation.
doomsday-book: A book compiled by order of William the Conqueror, containing a survey of all the lands in England. It consists of two volumes; a large folio, and a quarto. The folio contains 382 double pages of vellum, written in a small but plain character. The quarto contains 450 double pages of vellum, written in a large fair character.
domesday-book: A book compiled by order of William the Conqueror, containing a survey of all the lands in England. It consists of two volumes; a large folio, and a quarto. The folio contains 382 double pages of vellum, written in a small but plain character. The quarto contains 450 double pages of vellum, written in a large fair character.
door: An opening or passage into a house, or other building, or into any room, apartment or closet, by which persons enter. Such a passage is seldom or never called a gate.
The frame of boards, or any piece of board or plank that shuts the opening of a house or closes the entrance into an apartment or any inclosure, and usually turning on hinges.
In familiar language, a house; often in the pluralMy house is the first door from the corner. We have also the phrases, within doors, in the house; without doors, out of the house.
Entrance; as the door of life.

Avenue; passage; means of approach or access. An unforgiving temper shuts the door against reconciliation, or the door of reconciliation. I am the door; by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved. John 10:9. A door was opened to me of the Lord. 2 Corinthians 2:12. To lie at the door, in a figurative sense, is to be imputable or chargeable to one. If the thing is wrong, the fault lies at my door. Next door to, near to; bordering on. A riot unpunished is but next door to a tumult. Out of door or doors, quite gone; no more to be found. In doors, within the house; at home.
door-case: The frame which incloses a door.
door-keeper: A porter; one who guards the entrance of a house or apartment.
door-nail: The nail on which the knocker formerly struck.
door-stead: Entrance or place of a door.
doquet: Doket. A warrant; a paper granting license.
dor: The name of the black-beetle, or the hedgechafer, a species of Scarabaeus. We usually say, the dor-beetle.
dorr: The name of the black-beetle, or the hedgechafer, a species of Scarabaeus. We usually say, the dor-beetle.
dorado: A southern constellation, containing six stars, called also xiphias; not visible in our latitude.
A large fish resembling the dolphin.
doree: A fish of the genus Zeus. It is called also faber, and gallus marinus. The body is oval and greatly compressed on the sides; the head is large and the snout long.
doric: in general, pertaining to Doris, or the Dorians, in Greece, who dwelt near Parnassus. In architecture, noting the second order of columns, between the Tuscan and lonic. The Doric order is distinguished for simplicity and strength. It is used in the gates of cities and citadels, on the outside of churches. The Doric dialect of the Greek language was the dialect of the Dorians, and little different from that of Lacedemon. The Doric mode, in music, was the first of the authentic modes of the ancients. Its character is to be severe, tempered with gravity and joy.
doricism: A phrase of the Doric dialect.
dorism: A phrase of the Doric dialect.
dormant: Sleeping; hence, at rest; not in action; as dormant passions.
Being in a sleeping posture; as the lion dormant, in heraldry.
Neglected; not used; as a dormant title; dormant privileges.
Leaning; inclining; not perpendicular; as a dormant window, supposed to be so called form a beam of that name. This is now written dormer or dormar.
dormar: A window in the roof of a house, or above the entablature, being raised upon the rafters.
dormar-window: A window in the roof of a house, or above the entablature, being raised upon the rafters.
dormitive: A medicine to promote sleep; an opiate.
dormitory: A place, building or room to sleep in.
A gallery in convents divided into several cells, where the religious sleep.
dormouse: Dormice. An animal of the mouse kind, which makes a bed of moss or dry leaves, in a hollow tree or under shrubs, lays in a store of nuts or other food, and on the approach of cold weather, rolls itself in a ball and sleeps the greatest part of the winter.
dorsal: Pertaining to the back; as the dorsal fin of a fish; dorsal awn, in botany.
dorsiferous: In botany, bearing or producing seeds on the back of their leaves; an epithet given to ferns or plants of the capillary kind without stalks.
dorsiparous: In botany, bearing or producing seeds on the back of their leaves; an epithet given to ferns or plants of the capillary kind without stalks.
dose: The quantity of medicine give or prescribed to be taken at one time.
Any thing given to be swallowed; any thing nauseous, that one is obliged to take.
AS much as a man can swallow.
To proportion a medicine properly to the patient or disease; to form into suitable doses.
dosser: A pannier, or basket, to be carried on the shoulders of men.
dossil: In surgery, a pledget or portion of lint made into a cylindric form, or the shape of a date.
dost: the second person of do, used int he solemn style; thou dost.
dot: A small point or spot, made with a pen or other pointed instrument; a speck; used in marking a writing or other thing.
To mark or diversify with small detached objects; as a landscape dotted with cottages, or clumps of trees.
dotage: Feebleness or imbecility of understanding or mind, particularly in old age, childishness of old age; as a venerable man now in his dotage.
dotal: Pertaining to dower, or a womans marriage portion; constituting dower or comprised in it; as a dotal town.
dotard: A man whose intellect is impaired by age; one in his second childhood. The sickly dotard wants a wife.
A doting fellow; one foolishly fond.
dotation: The act of endowing, or of bestowing a marriage portion on a woman.
Endowment; establishment of funds for support; as of a hospital or eleemosynary corporation.
dote: To be delirious; to have the intellect impaired by age, so that the mind wanders or wavers; to be silly. Time has made you dote, and vainly tell of arms imagined in your lonely cell.

To be excessively in love; usually with on or upon; to dote on, is to love to excess or extravagance./ . What dust we dote on, when tis man we love. Aholah dotes on her lovers, the Assyrians. Ezekiel 23:5.
doter: One who dotes; a man whose understanding is enfeebled by age; a dotard.
One who is excessively fond, or weakly in love.
dottard: A tree kept low by cutting.
dotted: Marked with dots or small spots; diversified with small detached objects.
In botany, sprinkled with hollow dots or points.
dotterel: The name of different species of fowls, of the genus Charadrius and the grallic order; as the Alexandrine dotterel, the ringed dotterel, and the Morinellus; also, the turnstone or sea dotterel, a species of the genus Tringa.
dotting: Marking with dots or spots; diversifying with small detached objects.
double: Two of a sort together; one corresponding to the other; being in pairs; as double chickens in the same egg; double leaves connected by one petiole.

Twice as much; containing the same quantity or length repeated. Take double money in your hand. Genesis 43:12. Let a double portion of thy spirit be on me. 2 Kings 2:9. With to; as, the amount is double to what I expected.
Having one added to another; as a double chin.
double-banked: In seamanship, having two opposite oars managed by rowers on the same bench, or having two men to the same oar.
double-biting: Biting or cutting on either side; as a double-biting ax.
double-charge: To charge or intrust with a double portion.
double-dealer: One who acts two different parts, in the same business, or at the same time; a deceitful, trickish person; one who says one thing and thinks or intends another; one guilty of duplicity.
double-dealing: Artifice; duplicity; deceitful practice; the profession of one thing and the practice of another.
double-entendre: Double meaning of a word or expression.
double-face: Duplicity; the acting of different parts in the same concern.
double-headed: Having the flowers growing one to another.
double-hearted: a2. Having a false heart; deceitful; treacherous.
double-lock: To shoot the bolt twice; to fasten with double security.
double-manned: Furnished with twice the complement of men, or with two men instead of one.
double-minded: Having different minds at different times; unsettled; wavering; unstable; undetermined. James 1:8.
double-octave: In muxic, an interval composed of two octaves or fifteen notes in diatonic progression; a fifteenth.
double-plea: In law, a plea in which the defendant alledges two different matters in bar of the action.
double-quarrel: A complaint of a clerk to the archbishop against an inferior ordinary, for delay of justice.
double-shade: To double the natural darkness of a place.
double-threaded: Consisting of two threads twisted together.
double-tongued: Making contrary declarations on the same subject at different times; deceitful. The deacons must be grave, not double-tongued. 7 Timothy 3:8.
doubled: Folded; increased by adding an equal quantity, sum or value; repeated; turned or passed round.
doubler: An instrument for augmenting a very small quantity of electricity, so as to render it manifest by sparks or the electrometer.
doublet: The inner garment of a man; a waistcoat or vest.
Among lapidaries, a counterfeit stone composed of two pieces of crystal, with a color between them, so that they have the same appearance as if the whole substance of the crystal were colored.
doublets: The same number on both dice.
doubling: Making twice the sum, number or quantity; repeating; passing round; turning to escape.
The act of making double; also, a fold; a plait; also, an artifice; a shift.
doubloon: A Spanish and Portuguese coin, being double the value of the pistole.
doubly: In twice the quantity; to twice the degree; as doubly wise or good; to be doubly sensible of an obligation.
doubt: To waver or fluctuate in opinion; to hesitate; to be in suspense; to be in uncertainty; to be in suspense; to be in uncertainty, respecting the truth or fact; to be undetermined. Even in matters divine, concerning some things, we may lawfully doubt and suspend our judgment. So we say, I doubt whether it is proper; I doubt whether I shall go; sometimes with of, as we doubt of a fact.
To fear; to be apprehensive; to suspect. I doubt theres deep resentment in his mind.
To question, or hold questionable; to withhold assent from; to hesitate to believe; as, I have heard the story, but I doubt the truth of it.
doubted: Scrupled; questioned; not certain or settled.
doubter: One who doubts; one whose opinion is unsettled; one who scruples.
doubtful: Dubious; not settled in opinion; undetermined; wavering; hesitating; applied to persons; as, we are doubtful of a fact, or of the propriety of a measure.
Dubious; ambiguous; not clear in its meaning; as a doubtful expression.
Admitting of doubt; not obvious, clear or certain; questionable; not decided; as a doubtful case; a doubtful proposition; it is doubtful what will be the event of the war.
Of uncertain issue. We have sustained one day in doubtful fight.
Not secure; suspicious; as, we cast a doubtful eye.
doubtfully: Ambiguously; with uncertainty of I§=meaning. Nor did the goddess doubttfully declare.
doubtfulness: A state of doubt of uncertainty of mind; dubiousness; suspense; instability of opinion.
Uncertainty of event or issue; uncertainty of condition.
doubting: Wavering in mind; calling in question; hesitating.
doubtingly: In a doubting manner; dubiously; without confidence.
doubtless: Free from fear of danger; secure. Pretty child, sleep doubtless and secure.
Without doubt or question; unquestionably. The histories of Christ by the evangelists are doubtless authentic.
douceur: A present or gift; a bribe.
doucine: A molding concave above the convex below, serving as a cymatium to a delicate cornice; a gula.
doucker: A fowl that dips or dives in water.
dough: Do. Paste of bread; a mass composed of flour or meal moistened and kneaded, but not baked. My cake is dough, that is, by undertaking has not come to maturity: .
dough-baked: Unfinished; not hardened to perfection; soft.
dough-nut: A small roundish cake, made of flour, eggs and sugar, moistened with milk and boiled in lard.
doughty: Douty. Brave; valiant; eminent; noble; illustrious; as a doughty hero. It is now seldom used except in irony or burlesque.
doughy: Doy. Like dough; soft; yielding to pressure; pale.
douse: To thrust or plunge into water.
In seamens language, to strike or lower in haste; to slacken suddenly. Douse the top-sail.
douzeave: Doozeve. In music, a scale of twelve degrees.
dove: The oenas, or domestic pigeon, a species of Columba. Its color is a deep bluish ash color; the breast is dashed with a fine changeable green and purple; the sides of the neck, with a copper color. In a wild state, it builds its nest in holes of rocks or in hollow trees, but it is easily domesticated, and forms one of the luxuries of the table.
A word of endearment, or an emblem of innocence. Song of Solomon 2:14.
dove-cot: A small building or box in which domestic pigeons breed.
dove-house: A house or shelter for doves.
dove-tail: In carpentry, the manner of fastening boards and timbers together by letting one piece into another in the form of a doves tail spread, or wedge reversed, so that it cannot be drawn out. This is the strongest of all the fastenings or jointings.
To unite by a tenon in form of a pigeons tail spread, let into a board or timber.
dove-tailed: United by a tenon in form of a doves tail.
dowable: That may be endowed; entitled to dower.
dowager: A widow with a jointure; a title particularly given to the widows of princes and persons of rank. The widow of a king is called queen dowager.
dowcets: The testicles of a hart or stag.
dower: That portion of the lands or tenements of a man which his widow enjoys during her life, after the death of her husband.
The property which a woman brings to her husband in marriage.
The gift of a husband for a wife. Ask me never so much dowry and gift. Genesis 34:12.
Endowment; gift. How great, how plentiful, how rich a dower.
dowered: Furnished with dower, or a portion.
dowerless: Destitute of dower; having no portion or fortune.
dowery: A different spelling of dower, but little used, and they may well be neglected.
dowry: A different spelling of dower, but little used, and they may well be neglected.
The money, goods or estate which a woman brings to her husband in marriage; the portion given with a wife.
The reward paid for a wife.
dowlas: A kind of coarse linen cloth.
down: The fine soft feathers of fowls, particularly of the duck kind. The eider duck yields the best kind. Also, fine hair; as the down of the chin.
The pubescence of plants, a fine hairy substance.
The pappus or little crown of certain seeds of plants; a fine feathery or hairy substance by which seeds are conveyed to distance by the wind; as in dandelion and thistle.
Any thing that soothes or mollifies. Thou bosom softness; down of all my cares.
A bank or elevation of sand, thrown up by the sea.
downcast: Cast downward; directed to the ground; as a downcast eye or look, indicating bashfulness, modesty or dejection of mind.
downfall: A falling, or body of things falling; as the downfall of a flood.

Ruin; destruction; a sudden fall; or ruin by violence, in distinction from slow decay or declension; as the downfall of the Roman empire, occasioned by the conquests of the Northern nations; the downfall of a city; .
The sudden fall, depression or ruin of reputation or estate. We speak of the downfall of pride or glory, and of distinguished characters.
downgyved: Hanging down like the loose cincture of fetters.
down-haul: In seamens language, a rope passing along a stay, through the cringles of the stay-sail or jib, and made fast to the upper corner of the sail, to haul itdown.
downhill: Declivity; descent; slope. And though tis downhill all.
Declivous; descending; sloping. A downhill greensward. DOWWNHILL.
downlooked: Having a downcast countenance; dejected; gloomy; sullen; as jealousy downlooked.
downlying: The time of retiring to rest; time of repose.
About to be in travel of childbirth.
downright: Right down; — straight down; perpendicularly. A giant cleft downright.
In plain terms; without ceremony or circumlocution. We shall chide downright.
Completely; without stopping short; as, she fell downright into a fit.
Directly to the point; plain; open; artless; undisguised; as downright madness; downright nonsense; downright wisdom; downright falsehood; downright atheism.
Plain; artless; unceremonious; blunt; as, he spoke in his downright way.
down-sitting: The act of sitting down; repose; a resting. Thou knowest my down-sitting and my uprising. Psalm 139:2.
downward: From a higher place to a lower; in a descending course, whether directly toward the center of the earth, or not; as, to tend downward; to move or roll downwards; to look downward; to take root downwards.
In a course or direction from a head, spring, origin or source. Water flows downward toward the sea; we sailed downward on the stream.
In a course of lineal descent from an ancestor, considered as a head; as, to trace successive generations downward from Adam or Abraham.
In the course of falling or descending from elevation or distinction.
Moving or extending from a higher to a lower place, as on a slope or declivity, or in the open air; tending towards the earth or its center; as a downward course; he took his way with downward force.
downwards: From a higher place to a lower; in a descending course, whether directly toward the center of the earth, or not; as, to tend downward; to move or roll downwards; to look downward; to take root downwards.
In a course or direction from a head, spring, origin or source. Water flows downward toward the sea; we sailed downward on the stream.
In a course of lineal descent from an ancestor, considered as a head; as, to trace successive generations downward from Adam or Abraham.
In the course of falling or descending from elevation or distinction.
downy: Covered with down or nap; as a downy feather; downy wings.
Covered with pubescence or soft hairs, as a plant.
Made of down or soft feathers; as a downy pillow.
Soft, calm, soothing; as downy sleep.
doxological: Pertaining to doxology; giving praise to God.
doxology: In Christian worship, a hymn in praise of the Almighty; a particular form of giving glory to God.
doze: To slumber; to sleep lightly. If he happened to doze a little, the jolly cobbler waked him.
To live in a state of drowsiness; to be dull or half asleep; as, to doze away the time; to doze over a work.
To make dull; to stupefy. Dryden uses the participle dozed, Dozed with his fumes; but the transitive verb is seldom or never used.
dozen: Duzn. Twelve in number, applied to things of the same kind, but rarely or never to that number in the abstract. We say, a dozen men; a dozen pair of gloves. It is a word much used in common discourse and in light compositions; rarely in the grave or elevated style.
The number twelve of things of a like kind; as a dozen of eggs; twelve dozen of gloves; a dozen of wine.
dozy: Drowsy; heavy; inclined to sleep; sleepy; sluggish; as a dozy head.
drab: A kind of wooden box, used in salt works for holding the salt when taken out of the boiling pans. Its bottom is shelving or inclining that the water may drain off.
A kind of thick woolen cloth.
Being of a dun color, like the cloth so called.
drabble: To draggle; to make dirty by drawing in mud and water; to wet and befoul; as, to drabble a gown or cloke. In scottish, this word signifies to dirty by slabbering, as if it were allied to dribble, drivel, from the root of drip, which coincides with drop.
To fish for barbels with a long line and rod.
drabbling: Drawing in mud or water; angling for barbels.
A method of angling for barbels with a rod and a long line passed through a piece of lead.
drabler: In seamens language, a small additional sail, sometimes laced to the bottom of a bonnet on a square sail, in sloops and schooners. It is the same to a bonnet, as a bonnet is to a course.
drachma: A Grecian coin. Of the value of seven pence, three farthings, sterling, or nearly fourteen cents.
The eighth part of an ounce, or sixty grains, or three scruples; a weight used by apothecaries, but usually written dram.
draco: In astronomy, a constellation of the northern hemisphere, containing according to Flamstead, eighty stars.
A luminous exhalation from marshy grounds.
A genus of animals of two species.
dracontic: In astronomy, belonging to that space of time in which the moon performs one entire revolution.
dracunculus: In botany, a plant, a species of Arum, with a long stalk, spotted like a serpents belly .
In medicine, a long slender worm, bred in the muscular parts of the arms and legs called Guinea worm. These are troublesome in tropical climates, and are usually extirpated by the point of a needle.
drad: Terrible. This was also the old pret. of dread.
draff: Refuse; lees; dregs; the wash given to swine, or grains to cows; waste matter.
draft: A drawing; as, this horse is good for draft. In this sense, draught is perhaps most common.

A drawing of men from a military band; a selecting or detaching of soldiers from an army, or any part of it, or from a military post. Sometimes a drawing of men from other companies or societies. Several of the States had supplied the deficiency by drafts to serve for the year. These important posts, in consequence of heavy drafts, were left weakly defended.
An order from one man to another directing the payment of money; a bill of exchange. I thought it most prudent to defer the drafts, till advice was received of the progress of the loan.
A drawing of lines for a plan; a figure described on paper; delineation; sketch; plan delineated.
Depth of water necessary to float a ship.
draft-horse: A horse employed in drawing, particularly in drawing heavy loads or in plowing.
drag: To pull; to haul; to draw along the ground by main force; applied particularly to drawing heavy things with labor, along the ground or other surface; as, to drag stone or timber; to drag a net in fishing. John 21:8.
To break land by drawing a drag or harrow over it; to harrow; a common use of this word in New England.
To draw along slowly or heavily; to draw any thing burdensome; as, to drag a lingering life.
To draw along in contempt, as unworthy to be carried. He drags me at his chariot-wheels. To drag one in chains.
To pull or haul about roughly and forcibly. In seamens language, to drag an anchor, is to draw or trail it along the bottom when loosened, or when the anchor will not hold the ship.
dragged: Drawn on the ground; drawn with labor or force; drawn along slowly and heavily; raked with a drag or harrow.
dragging: Drawing on the ground; drawing with labor or by force; drawing slowly or heavily; raking with a drag.
draggle: To wet and dirty by drawing on the ground or mud, or on wet grass; to drabble.
To be drawn on the ground; to become wet or dirty by being drawn on the mud or wet grass.
draggled: Drawn on the ground; wet or dirtied by being drawn on the ground or mire.
draggling: Drawing on the ground; making dirty by drawing on the ground or wet grass.
dragman: A fisherman that uses a dragnet.
dragnet: A net to be drawn on the bottom of a river or pond for taking fish.
dragoman: An interpreter; a term in general use in the Levant and other parts of the East.
drogman: An interpreter; a term in general use in the Levant and other parts of the East.
dragon: A genus of animals, the Draco. They have four legs, a cylindrical tail, and membranaceous wings, radiated like the fins of a flying-fish.
A kind of winged serpent, much celebrated in the romances of the middle ages.
A fiery, shooting meteor, or imaginary serpent. Swift, swift, ye dragons of the night! That dawning may bear the ravens eye.
A fierce, violent person, male or female; as, this man or woman is a dragon.

A constellation of the northern hemisphere. In Scripture, dragon seems sometimes to signify a large marine fish or serpent, Isaiah 27:7. Where the leviathan is also mentioned; also Psalm 74:13. Sometimes it seems to signify a venomous land serpent. Psalm 91:13. The dragon shalt thou trample under foot. It is often used for the devil, who is called the old serpent. Revelation 20:2,
dragonet: A fish with a slender round body, colored with yellow, blue and white; the head is large and depressed at the top and has two orifices, through which it breathes and ejects water, like the cetaceous tribe.
dragon-fish: A species of Trachinus, called the weaver. This fish is about twelve inches in length; it has two or three longitudinal lines of a dirty yellow on the sides, and the belly of a silvery hue. The wounds of its spines occasion inflammation. It buries itself int he sand, except its nose.
dragon-fly: A genus of insects, the Libella or Libellula, having four extended wings; they are furnished with jaws; the antennae are shorter than the thorax; and the tail of the male is terminated by a kind of hooked forceps. There are many species, with a great diversity of colors.
dragonish: In the form of a dragon; dragonlike.
dragons: A genus of plants, the Dracontium, of several species, natives of the Indies.
dragon-shell: A species of concamerated patella or limpet. The top is much curved, and of an ash-color on the outside, but internally, of a bright flesh color. It is found adhering to larger shells, or to the back of the tortoise, as common limpets do to the sides of rocks.
dragoon: A soldier or musketeer who serves on horseback or on foot, as occasion may require. Their arms are a sword, a musket and a bayonet.
To persecute by abandoning a place to the rage of soldiers.
To enslave or reduce to subjection by soldiers.
To harass; to persecute; to compel to submit by violent measures; to force. The colonies may be influenced to any thing, but they can de dragooned to nothing.
dragoonade: The abandoning of a place to the rage of soldiers.
dragooned: Abandoned to the violence of soldiers; persecuted; harassed.
dragooning: Abandoning to the rage of soldiers; persecuting; harassing; vexing.
drain: To filter; to cause to pass through some porous substance. Salt water, drained through twenty vessels of earth, hath become fresh.
To empty or clear of liquor, by causing the liquor to drop or run off slowly; as, to drain a vessel or its contents.
To make dry; to exhaust of water or other liquor, by causing it to flow off in channels, or through porous substances; as, to drain land; to drain a swamp or marsh.
To empty; to exhaust; to draw off gradually; as, a foreign war drains a country of specie.
To flow off gradually; as, let the water of low ground drain off.
drainage: A draining; a gradual flowing off of any liquid.
drained: Emptied of water or other liquor by a gradual discharge, flowing or dropping; exhausted; drawn off.
draining: Emptying of water or other liquor by filtration or flowing in small channels.
drake: The male of the duck kind.
dram: Among druggists and physicians, a weight of the eighth part of an ounce, or sixty grains. In avoirdupois weight, the sixteenth part of an ounce.
A small quantity; as no dram of judgment.
AS much spirituous liquor as is drank at once; as a dram of brandy. Drams are the slow poison of life.
To drink drams; to indulge in the use of ardent spirit.
drama: A poem or composition representing a picture of human life, and accommodated to action. The principal species of the drama are tragedy and comedy; inferior species are tragi-comedy, opera
dramatic: Pertaining to the drama; represent by action; theatrical; not narrative.
dramatical: Pertaining to the drama; represent by action; theatrical; not narrative.
dramatically: By representation; in the manner of the drama.
dramatist: The author of a dramatic composition; a writer of plays.
dramatize: To compose in the form of the drama; or to give to a composition the form of a play. At Riga in 1204 was acted a prophetic play, that is, a dramatized extract from the history of the Old and New Testaments.
dramseller: One who sells distilled liquors by the dram or glass.
dramshop: A shop or barroom where spirits are sold by the dram.
drape: To make cloth; also, to banter.
draper: One who sells cloths; a dealer in cloths; as a linen-draper or woolen-draper.
drapery: Clothwork; the trade of making cloth.
In sculpture and painting, the representation of the clothing or dress of human figures; also, tapestry, hangings, curtains.
drastic: Powerful; acting with strength or violence; efficacious; as a drastic cathartic.
draught: The act of drawing; as a horse or ox fit for draught.
The quality of being drawn; as a cart of plow of easy draught.
The drawing of liquor into the mouth and throat; the act of drinking.
The quantity of liquor drank at once.
The act of delineating, or that which is delineated; a representation by lines, as the figure of a house, a machine, a fort, described on paper.
draught-hooks: Large hooks of iron fixed on the cheeks of a cannon carriage, two on each side, one near the trunnion hole, and the other at the train; used in drawing the gun backwards and forwards by means of draught ropes.
draught-horse: A horse used in drawing a plow, cart or other carriage, as distinguished from a saddle horse.
draught-house: A house for the reception of filth or waste matter.
draughtsman: A man who draws writings or designs or one who is skilled in such drawings. DRAUGHT SMAN.
One who drinks drams; a tippler.
drave: the old participle of drive. We now use drove.
draw: To pull along; to haul; to cause to move forward by force applied in advance of the thing moved or at the fore-end, as by a rope or chain. It differs from drag only in this, that drag is more generally applied to things moved along the ground by sliding, or moved with greater toil or difficulty, and draw is applied to all bodies moved by force in advance, whatever may be the degree of force. Draw is the more general or generic term, and drag, more specific. We say, the horses draw a coach or wagon, but they drag it through mire; yet draw is properly used in both cases.
To pull out, as to draw a sword or dagger from its sheath; to unsheathe. Hence, to draw the sword, is to wage war.
To bring by compulsion; to cause to come. Do not rich men oppress you, and draw you before the judgment seat? James 2:6.
To pull up or out; to raise from any depth; as, to draw water from a well.
drawback: Money or an amount paid back. Usually, a certain amount of duties or customs, paid or bonded by an importer, paid back or remitted to him on the exportation of the goods; or a certain amount of excise paid back or allowed on the exportation of home manufacturers.
In popular sense, any loss of advantage, or deduction from profit .
draw-bridge: A bridge which may be drawn up or let down to admit or hinder communication, as before the gate of a town or castle, or in a bridge over a navigable river. In the latter, the draw-bridge usually consists of two movable platforms, which may be raised to let a vessel pass through.
draw-net: A net for catching the larger sorts of fowls, made of pack-thread, with wide meshes.
draw-well: A deep well, from which water is drawn by a long cord or pole.
drawee: The person on whom an order or bill of exchange is drawn; the payer of a bill or exchange.
drawer: One who draws or pulls; one who takes water from a well; one who draws liquors from a cask.
That which draws or attracts, or has the power of attraction.
He who draws a bill of exchange or an order for the payment of money.
A sliding box in a case or table, which is drawn at pleasure.
Drawers, in the plural, a close under garment worn on the lower limbs.
drawing: The act of pulling, hauling, or attracting.
The act of representing the appearance or figures of objects on a plain surface, by means of lines and shades, as with a pencil, crayon, pen, compasses; delineation.
drawing-master: One who teaches the art of drawing.
drawing-room: A room appropriated for the reception of company; a room in which distinguished personages hold levees, or private persons receive parties. It is written by Coxe, withdrawing-room, a room to which company withdraws from the dining-room.
The company assembled in a drawing-room.
drawl: To utter words in a slow lengthened tone.
A lengthened utterance of the voice.
drawn: Pulled; hauled; allured; attracted; delineated; extended; extracted; derived; deduced; written.
Equal, where each party takes his own stake; as a drawn game.
Having equal advantage, and neither party a victory; as a drawn battle.
Moved aside, as a curtain; unclosed, or closed.
dray: A low cart or carriage on wheels, drawn by a horse.
dray-horse: A horse used for drawing a dray.
dray-man: A man who attends a dray.
drazel: Drazl. A dirty woman; a slut.
dread: Great fear, or apprehension of evil or danger. It expresses more than fear, and less than terror or fright. It is an uneasiness or alarm excited by expected pain, loss or other evil. We speak of the dread of evil; the dread of suffering; the dread of the divine displeasure. It differs from terror also in being less sudden or more continued.
Terror. Shall not his dread fall on you. Job 13:11.
The cause of fear; the person or the thing dreaded. Let him be your dread. Isaiah 8:73.
dreader: One that fears, or lives in fear.
dreadful: Impressing great fear; terrible; formidable; as a dreadful storm, or dreadful night. The great and dreadful day of the Lord. Malachi 4:5.
Awful; venerable. How dreadful is this place. Genesis 28:17.
dreadfully: Terribly; in a manner to be dreaded.
dreadfulness: Terribleness; the quality of being dreadful; frightfulness.
dreadless: Fearless; bold; not intimidated; undaunted; free from fear or terror; intrepid.
dreadlessness: Fearlessness; undauntedness; freedom from fear or terror; boldness.
dream: The thought or series of thoughts of a person in sleep. We apply dream, in the singular, to a series of thoughts, which occupy the mind of a sleeping person, in which he imagines he has a view of real things or transactions. A dream is a series of thoughts not under the command of reason, and hence wild and irregular.

In scripture, dreams were sometimes impressions on the minds of sleeping persons, made by divine agency. God came to Abimelech in a dream. Joseph was warned by God in a dream. Genesis 20:3; Matthew 2:13.
A vain fancy; a wild conceit; an unfounded suspicion.
To have ideas or images in the mind, in the state of sleep; with of before a noun; as, to dream of a battle; to dream of an absent friend.
dreamer: A fanciful man; a visionary; one who forms or entertains vain schemes; as a political dreamer.
A man lost in wild imagination; a mope; a sluggard.
dreaming: Having thoughts or ideas in sleep.
drear: Dismal; gloomy with solitude. A drear and dying sound.
dreary: Dismal; gloomy; as a dreary waste; dreary shades. This word implies both solitude and gloom.
dredge: A mixture of oats and barley sown together.
To take, catch or gather with a dredge.
To sprinkle flour on roast meat.
dredger: One who fishes with a dredge; also, an utensil for scattering flour on meat while roasting. DREDGING-BOxA box used for dredging meat.
dredging-machine: An engine used to take up mud or gravel from the bottom of rivers, docks
dregginess: Fullness of dregs or lees; foulness; feculence.
dreggish: Full of dregs; foul with lees; feculent.
dreggy: Containing dregs or lees; consisting of dregs; foul; muddy; feculent.
dregs: The sediment of liquors; lees; grounds; feculence; any foreign matter of liquors that subsides to the bottom of a vessel.
Waste or worthless matter; dross; sweepings; refuse. Hence, the most vile and despicable part of men; as the dregs of society. Dreg, in the singular, is found in Spenser, but is not now used.
drench: To wet thoroughly; to soak; to fill or cover with water or other liquid; as garments drenched in rain or in the sea; the flood has drenched the earth; swords drenched in blood.
A draught; a swill; also, a portion of medicine to purge a beast, particularly a horse. Hence, a violent dose of physic to be forced down the throat.
drenched: Soaked; thoroughly wet; purged with a dose.
drencher: One who wets or steeps; one who gives a drench to a beast.
dress: pret. and pp. dressed or drest.
To make straight or a straight line; to adjust to a right line. We have the primary sense in the military phrase, dress your ranks. Hence the sense, to put in order.
To adjust; to put in good order; as, to dress the beds of a garden. Sometimes, to till or cultivate. Genesis 2:15; Deuteronomy 28:39.
To put in good order, as a wounded limb; to cleanse a wound, and to apply medicaments. The surgeon dresses the limb or the wound.

To prepare, in a general sense; to put in the condition desired; to make suitable or fit; as, to dress meat; to dress leather or cloth; to dress a lamp; but we, in the latter case, generally use trim. To dress hemp or flax, is to break and clean it.
dressed: Adjusted; made straight; put in order; prepared; trimmed; tilled; clothed; adorned; attired.
dresser: One who dresses; one who is employed in putting on clothes and adorning another; one who is employed in preparing trimming or adjusting any thing.
A side-board; a table or bench on which meat and other things are dressed or prepared for use.
dressing: Adjusting to a line; putting in order; preparing; clothing; embellishing; cultivating.
That which is used as an application to a wound or sore.
That which is used in preparing land for a drop; manure spread over land. When it remains on the surface, it is called a top- dressing.
In popular language, correction; a flogging, or beating.
dressing-room: An apartment appropriated for dressing the person.
dress-maker: A maker of gowns, or similar garments; a mantuamaker.
dressy: Showy in dress; wearing rich or showy dresses.
dreul: To emit saliva; to suffer saliva to issue and flow down from the mouth.
drib: To crop or cut off; to defalcate.
dribble: To fall in drops or small drops, or in a quick succession of drops; as, water dribbles from the eaves.
To slaver as a child or an idiot.
To fall weakly and slowly; as the dribbling dart of love.
dribblet: A small piece or part; a small sum; odd money in a sum; as, the money was paid in dribblets.
dribbling: Falling in drops or small drops.
dried: Of dry. Free from moisture or sap.
drier: That which has the quality of drying; that which may expel or absorb moisture; a desiccative. The sun and northwesterly wind are great driers of the earth.
drift: That which is driven by wind or water, as drift seems to be primarily a participle. Hence,
A heap of any matter driven together; as a drift of snow, called also a snow-drift; a drift of sand.
A driving; a force impelling or urging forward; impulse; overbearing power or influence; as the drift of a passion.
Course of any thing; tendency; aim; main force; as the drift of reasoning or argument; the drift of a discourse.
Any thing driven by force, as a drift of dust; a log or a raft driven by a stream of water, without guidance.
drifting: Driving by force; driving into heaps.
drift-sail: In navigation, a sail used under water, veered out right ahead by sheets.
drift-way: A common way for driving cattle in.
drift-wind: A driving wind; a wind that drives things into heaps.
drill: To pierce with a drill; to perforate by turning a sharp pointed instrument of a particular form; to bore and make a hole by turning an instrument. We say, to drill a hole through a piece of metal, or to drill a cannon.
To draw on; to entice; to amuse and put off. She drilled him on to five and fifty.
To draw on from step to step.
To draw through; to drain; as, waters drilled through a sandy stratum.
In a military sense, to teach and train raw soldiers to their duty, by frequent exercise; a common and appropriate use of the word.
drilled: Bored or perforated with a drill; exercised; sown in rows.
drilling: Boring with a drill; training to military duty; sowing in drills.
drill-plow: A plow for sowing grain in drills.
drink: pret. and pp. drank. Old pret. and pp. drunk; pp. Drunken.
To swallow liquor, for quenching thirst or other purpose; as, to drink of the brook. Ye shall indeed drink of my cup. Matthew 20:23.
To take spirituous liquors to excess; to be intemperate in the use of spirituous liquors; to be a habitual drunkard.
To feast; to be entertained with liquors. To drink to,
To salute in drinking; to invite to drink by drinking first; as, I drink to you grace.
drinkable: That may be drank; fit or suitable for drink; potable.
drinker: One who drinks, particularly one who practices drinking spirituous liquors to excess; a drunkard; a tipler.
drinking: The act of swallowing liquors, or of absorbing.
The practice of drinking to excess. We say, a man is given to drinking.
drinking-horn: A horn cup, such as our rude ancestors used.
drinking-house: A house frequented by tiplers; an alehouse.
drink-money: Money given to buy liquor for drink.
drip: To fall in drops; as, water drips from eaves.
To have any liquid falling from it in drops; as, a wet garment drips.
To let fall in drops. The thatch drips fast a shower of rain. So we say, roasting flesh drips fat.
A falling in drops, or that which falls in drops. In building, avoid the drip of your neighbors house.
The edge of a roof; the eaves; a large flat member of the cornice.
dripping: Falling or letting fall in drops.
The fat which falls from meat in roasting; that which falls in drops.
dripping-pan: A pan for receiving the fat which drips from meat in roasting.
drive: To impel or urge forward by force; to force; to move by physical force. We drive a nail into wood with a hammer; the wind or a current drive a ship on the ocean.

To compel or urge forward by other means than absolute physical force, or by means that compel the will; as, to drive cattle to market. A smoke drives company from the room. A man may be drive by the necessities of the times, to abandon his country. Drive thy business; let not thy business drive thee.
To chase; to hunt. To drive the deer with hound and horn.

To impel a team of horses or oxen to move forward, and to direct their course; hence, to guide or regulate the course of the carriage drawn by them. We say, to drive a team, or to drive a carriage drawn by a team.
drivel: To slaver; to let spittle drop or flow from the mouth, like a child, idiot or dotard.
To be weak or foolish; to dote; as a driveling hero; driveling love.
Slaver; saliva flowing from the mouth.
A driveller; a fool; an idiot.
driveler: A slaverer; a slabberer; an idiot; a fool.
driven: Drivn. Urged forward by force; impelled to move; constrained by necessity.
driver: One who drives; the person or thing that urges or compels any thing else to move.
The person who drives a carriage; one who conducts a team.
A large sail occasionally set on the mizenyard or gaff, the foot being extended over the stern by a boom.
drizzle: To rain in small drops; to fall as water from the clouds in very fine particles. We say, it drizzles; drizzling drops; drizzling rain; drizzling tears.
To shed in small drops or particles. The air doth drizzle dew. Winters drizzled snow.
drizzled: Shed or thrown down in small drops or particles.
drizzling: Falling in fine drops or particles; shedding in small drops or particles.
The falling of rain or snow in small drops.
drizzly: Shedding small rain, or small particles of snow. The winters drizzly reign.
droil: To work sluggishly or slowly; to plod.
A mope; a drone; a sluggard; a drudge.
droll: Odd; merry; facetious; comical; as a droll fellow.
One whose occupation or practice is to raise mirth by odd tricks; a jester; a buffoon.
A farce; something exhibited to raise mirth or sport.
To jest; to play the buffoon.
drollery: Sportive tricks; buffoonery; comical stories; gestures, manners or tales adapted to raise mirth.
dromedary: A species of camel, called also the Arabian camel, with one bunch or protuberance on the back, in distinction from the Bactrian camel, which has two bunches. It has four callous protuberances on the fore legs, and two on the hind ones. It is a common beast of burden in Egypt, Syria, and the neighboring countries.
drone: The male of the honey bee. It is smaller than the queen bee, but larger than the working bee. The drones make no honey, but after living a few weeks, they are killed or driven from the hive. Hence,
An idler; a sluggard; one who earns nothing by industry.
A humming or low sound, or the instrument of humming.
The largest tube of the bag-pipe, which emits a continued deep note.
To live in idleness; as a droning king.
drone-ely: A two-winged insect, resembling the drone-bee.
droning: Living in idleness; giving a dull sound.
dronish: Idle; sluggish; lazy; indolent; inactive; slow.
droop: To sink or hang down; to lean downwards, as a body that is weak or languishing. Plants droop for want of moisture; the human body droops in old age or infirmity.
To languish from grief or other cause.
To fail or sink; to decline; as, the courage or the spirits droop.
To faint; to grow weak; to be dispirited; as, the soldiers droop from fatigue.
drooping: Sinking; hanging or leaning downward; declining; languishing; failing.
drop: Asmall portion of any fluid in a spherical form, which falls at once from any body, or a globule of any fluid which is pendent, as if about to fall; a small portion of water falling in rain; as a drop of water; a drop of blood; a drop of laudanum.
A diamond hanging from the ear; an earring; something hanging in the form of a drop.
A very small quantity of liquor; as, he had not drank a drop.
The part of a gallows which sustains the criminal before he is executed, and which is suddenly dropped.
To distill; to fall in small portions, globules or drops, as a liquid. Water drops from the clouds or from the eaves.
drops: To pour or let fall in small portions or globules, as a fluid; to distill. The heavens shall drop down dew. Deuteronomy 33:28.
To let fall as any substance; as, to drop the anchor; to drop a stone.
To let go; to dismiss; to lay aside; to quit; to leave; to permit to subside; as, to drop an affair; to drop a controversy; to drop a pursuit.
To utter slightly, briefly or casually; as, to drop a word in favor of a friend.
To insert indirectly, incidentally, or by way of digression; as, to drop a word of instruction in a letter .
drop-serene: A disease of the eye; amaurosis, or blindness from a diseased retina.
drop-stone: Spar in the shape of drops.
drop-wort: The name of a plant, the Spiraea filipendula. The hemlock drop-wort, and the water drop-wort, are species of Oenanthe.
dropped: Let fall; distilled; laid aside; dismissed; let go; suffered to subside; sprinkled or variegated.
dropping: Falling in globules; distilling; falling; laying aside; dismissing; quitting; suffering to rest or subside; variegating with ornaments like drops.
The act of dropping; a distilling; a falling.
dropsical: Diseased with dropsy; hydropical; inclined to the dropsy; applied to persons.
Partaking of the nature of the dropsy; applied to disease.W .
dropsy: In medicine, an unnatural collection of water, in an part of the body, proceeding from a greater effusion of serum by the exhalant arteries, than the absorbents take up. It occurs most frequently in persons of lax habits, or in bodies debilitated by disease. The dropsy takes different names, according to the part affected; as ascites, or dropsy of the abdomen; hydrocephalus, or water in the head; anasarca, or a watery swelling over the whole body; etc.
dross: The recrement or despumation of metals; the scum or extraneous matter of metals, thrown off in the process of melting.
Rust; crust of metals; an incrustation formed on metals by oxydation.
Waste matter; refuse; any worthless matter separated from the better part; impure matter. The worlds glory is but dross unclean.
drossiness: Foulness; rust; impurity; a state of being drossy.
drossy: Full of dross; abounding with scorious or recrementitious matter; as drossy gold.
drouth: Dryness; want of rain or of water; particularly, dryness of the weather, which affects the earth, and prevents the growth of plants; aridness; aridity.
Dryness of the throat and mouth; thirst; want of drink.
drouthiness: A state of dryness of the weather; want of rain.
drouthy: Dry, as the weather; arid; wanting rain.
drove: A collection of cattle driven; a number of animals, as oxen, sheep or swine, driven in a body. We speak of a herd of cattle, and a flock of sheep, when a number is collected; but properly a drove is a herd or flock driven. It is applicable to any species of brutes. Hence,
Any collection of irrational animals, moving or driving forward; as a finny drove.
A crowd of people in motion. Where droves, as ata city gate, may pass.
drover: One who drives cattle or sheep to market. Usually in New England, a man who makes it his business to purchase fat cattle and drive them to market.
A boat driven by the tide.
drown: Literally, to overwhelm in water; an appropriately, to extinguish life by immersion in water or other fluid; applied to animals; also, to suspend animation by submersion.
To overwhelm in water; as, to drown weeds.
To overflow; to deluge; to inundate; as, to drown land.
To immerse; to plunge and lose; to overwhelm; as, to drown ones self in sensual pleasure.
To overwhelm; to overpower. My private voice is drowned amid the senate.
drowned: Deprived of life by immersion in a fluid; overflowed; inundated; overwhelmed.
drowning: Destroying life by submersion in a liquid; overflowing; overwhelming.
drowse: To sleep imperfectly or unsoundly; to slumber; to be heavy with sleepiness.
To look heavy; to be heavy or dull.
To make heavy with sleep; to make dull or stupid.
drowsiness: Sleepiness; heaviness with sleep; disposition to sleep.
drowsy: Inclined to sleep; sleepy; heavy with sleepiness; lethargic; comatose.
Disposing to sleep; lulling; as a drowsy couch.
drub: é To beat with a stick; to thrash; to cudgel. The little thief had been soundly drubbed with a cudgel.
A blow with a stick or cudgel; a thump; a knock.
drubbed: Beat with a cudgel; beat soundly.
drubbing: Beating with a cudgel; beating soundly.
drudge: druj. To work hard; to labor in mean offices; to labor with toil and fatigue. In merriment did drudge and labor.
One who works hard, or labors with toil and fatigue; one who labors hard in servile employments; a slave.
drudgery: Hard labor; toilsome work; ignoble toil; hard work in servile occupations. Paradise was a place of bliss-without drudgery or sorrow./\ .
drug: The general name of substances used in medicine, sold by the druggist, and compounded by apothecaries and physicians; any substance, vegetable, animal or mineral, which is used in the composition or preparation of medicines. It is also applied to dyeing materials.
Any commodity that lies on hand, or is not salable; an article of slow sale, or in no demand in market.
A mortal drug, or a deadly drug, is poison.
To prescribe or administer drugs or medicines.
drugget: A cloth or thin stuff of wool, or of wool and thread, corded or plain, usually plain.
druggist: One who deals in drugs; properly, one whose occupation is merely to but and sell drugs, without compounding or preparation. In America, the same person often carries on the business of the druggist and the apothecary.
druid: A priest or minister of religion, among the ancient Celtic nations in Gaul, Britain and Germany. The Druids possessed some knowledge of geometry, natural philosophy, superintended the affairs of religion and morality, and performed the office of judges.
druidism: The system of religion, philosophy and instruction taught by the druids, or their doctrines, rites and ceremonies.
drum: A martial instrument of music, in form of a hollow cylinder, and covered at the ends with vellum, which is stretched or slackened at pleasure.
In machinery, a short cylinder revolving on an axis, generally for the purpose of turning several small wheels, by means of straps passing round its periphery.

The drum of the ear, the tympanum, or barrel of the ear; the hollow part of the ear, behind the membrane of the tympanum. The latter is a tense membrane, which closes the external passage of the ear, and receives the vibrations of the air.
To beat a drum with sticks; to beat or play a tune on a drum.
To beat with the fingers, as with drumsticks; to beat with a rapid succession of strokes; as, to drum on the table.
drum-fish: A fish, found on the coast of North America.
drum-major: The chief or first drummer of a regiment.
drummer: One whose office is to beat the drum, in military exercises and marching; one who drums.
drum-stick: The stick with which a drum is beaten, or shaped for the purpose of beating a drum.
drunk: Intoxicated; inebriated; overwhelmed or overpowered by spirituous liquor; stupefied or inflamed by the action of spirit on the stomach and brain. It is brutish to be drunk. Be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess.
Drenched or saturated with moisture or liquor. I will make my arrows drunk with blood. Deuteronomy 32:42.
drunkard: One given to ebriety or an excessive used of strong liquor; a person who habitually or frequently is drunk. A drunkard and a glutton shall come to poverty. Proverbs 23:21.
drunken: Given to drunkenness; as a drunken butler.
Saturated with liquor or moisture; drenched. Let the earth be drunken with our blood.
Proceeding from intoxication; done in a state of drunkenness; as a drunken quarrel. A drunken slaughter.
drunkenness: Intoxication; inebriation; a state in which a person is overwhelmed or overpowered with spirituous liquors, so that his reason is disordered, and he reels or staggers in walking. Drunkenness renders some persons stupid, others gay, others sullen, others furious. Let us walk honestly as in the day; not in rioting and drunkenness.
Disorder of the faculties resembling intoxication by liquors; inflammation; frenzy; rage. Passion is the drunkenness of the mind.
drupe: In botany, a pulpy pericarp or fruit without valves, containing a nut or stone with a kernel; as the plum, cherry, apricot, peach, almond, olive
drupaceous: Pertaining to drupes; or consisting of drupes; as drupaceous fruit; drupaceous follicles.
druse: Among miners, a cavity in a rock having its interior surface studded with crystals, or filled with water.
drusy: s as z. Abounding with very minute crystals; as a drusy surface.
dry: Destitute of moisture; free from water or wetness; arid; not moist; as dry land; dry clothes.
Not rainy; free from rain or mist; as dry weather; a dry March or April.
Not juicy; free from juice, sap or aqueous matter; not green; as dry wood; dry stubble; dry hay; dry leaves.
Without tears; as dry eyes; dry mourning.
Not giving milk; as, the cow is dry.
dryad: In mythology, a deity or nymph of the woods; a nymph supposed to preside over woods.
dryer: He or that which dries; that which exhausts of moisture or greenness.
dryeyed: Not having tears in the eyes.
dryfoot: A dog that pursues game by the scent of the foot.
drying: Expelling or losing moisture, sap or greenness.
The act or process of depriving of moisture or greenness.
dryite: Fragments of petrified or fossil wood in which the structure of the wood is recognized.
dryly: Barrenly; without embellishment; without any thing to enliven, enrich or entertain.
dryness: Destitution of moisture; want of water or other fluid; siccity; a aridity; aridness; as the dryness of a soil; dryness of the road.
Want of rain; as dryness of weather.
Want of juice or succulence; as dryness of the bones or fibers.
Want of succulence or greenness; as the dryness of hay or corn.
Barrenness; jejuneness; want of ornament or pathos; want of that which enlivens and entertains; as the dryness of style or expression; the dryness of a subject.
drynurse: Anurse who attends and feeds a child without the breast.
One who attends another in sickness.
To feed, attend and bring up without the breast.
dryrub: To rub and cleanse without wetting.
drysalter: A dealer in salted or dry meats, pickles, sauces
dual: Expressing the number two; as the dual number in Greek.
dualistic: Consisting of two. The dualistic system of Anaxagoras and Plato taught that there are two principles in nature, one active, the other passive.
duality: That which expresses two in number.
The state or quality of being two.
dub: To strike a blow with a sword, and make a knight. Se cyng, dubbade his sunu Henric to ridere. The King dubbed his son Henry a knight.
To confer any dignity or new character. A man of wealth is dubbd a man of worth.
dubious: Doubtful; wavering or fluctuating in opinion; not settled; not determined; as, the mind is in a dubious state.
Uncertain; that of which the truth is not ascertained or known; as a dubious question.
Not clear; not plain; as dubious light.
Of uncertain event or issue. In dubious battle.
dubiousness: Doubtfulness; a state of wavering and indecision of mind; as, he speaks with dubiousness.
Uncertainty; as the dubiousness of the question.
dubitable: Doubtful; uncertain. But the derivative indubitable is often used.
ducal: Pertaining to a duke; as a ducal coronet.
ducat: A coin of several countries in Europe, struck in the dominions of a duke. It is of silver or gold. The silver ducat is generally of the value of four shillings and sixpence sterling, equal to an American dollar, or to a French crown, and the gold ducat of twice the value.
ducatoon: A silver coin, struck chiefly in Italy, of the value of about four shillings and eight pence sterling, or nearly 104 cents. The gold ducatoon of Holland is worth twenty florins.
duchess: The consort or widow of a duke. Also, a lady who has the sovereignty of a duchy.
duchy: The territory or dominions of a duke; a dukedom; as the duchy of Lancaster.
duchy-court: The court of the duchy of Lancaster in England.
duck: A species of coarse cloth or canvas, used for sails, sacking of beds
A water fowl, so called from its plunging. There are many species or varieties of the duck, some wild, others tame.
An inclination of the head, resembling the motion of a duck in water.
A stone thrown obliquely on the water so as to rebound; as in duck and drake.
A word of endearment or fondness.
ducker: A plunger; a diver; a cringer.
ducking: Plunging; thrusting suddenly into water and withdrawing; dipping.

The act of plunging or putting in water and withdrawing. Ducking is a punishment of offenders in France, and among English seamen, it is a penalty to which sailors are subject on passing, for the first time, the equator or tropic.
ducking-stool: A stool or chair in which common scolds were formerly tied and plunged into water.
duck-legged: Having short legs, like a duck.
duck-meat: A plant, the Lemna, growing in ditches and shallow water, and serving for food for ducks and geese. The starry ducks-meat is the Callitriche.
duct: Any tube or canal by which fluid or other substance is conducted or conveyed. It is particularly used to denote the vessels of an animal body, by which the blood, chyle, lymph, are carried from one part to another, and the vessels of plants in which the sap is conveyed.
ductile: That may be led; easy to be led or drawn; tractable; complying; obsequious; yielding to motives, persuasion or instruction; as the ductile minds of youth; a ductile people.
Flexible; pliable. The ductile rind and leaves of radiant gold.
That may be drawn out into wire or threads. Gold is the most ductile of the metals.
That may be extended by beating.
ductileness: The quality of suffering extension by drawing or percussion; ductility.
ductility: The property of solid bodies, particularly metals, which renders them capable of being extended by drawing without breaking; as the ductility of gold, iron or brass.
Flexibility; obsequiousness; a disposition of mind that easily yields to motives or influence; ready compliance.
due: Owed; that ought to be paid or done to another. That is due from me to another which contract, justice or propriety requires me to pay, and which he may justly claim as his right. Reverence is due to the creator; civility is due from one man to another. Money is due at the expiration of the credit given, or at the period promised.
Proper; fit; appropriate; suitable; becoming; required by the circumstances; as, the event was celebrated with due solemnities. Men seldom have a due sense of their depravity.
Seasonable; as, he will come in due time.
Exact; proper; as, the musicians keep due time.
duel: Single combat; a premeditated combat between two persons, for the purpose of deciding some private difference or quarrel. A sudden fight, not premeditated, is called a rencounter. A duel is fought with deadly weapons and with a purpose to take life.
dueling: The act or practice of fighting in single combat.
duelist: One who fights in single combat. The duelist values his honor above the life of his antagonist, his own life, and the happiness of his family.
One who professes to study the rules of honor.
duenna: An old woman who is kept to guard a younger; a governess.
duet: A song or air in two parts.
duetto: A song or air in two parts.
duffel: A kind of coarse woolen cloth, having a thick nap or frieze.
dug: The pap or nipple of a cow or other beast. It is applied to a human female in contempt, but seems to have been used formerly of the human breast without reproach. From tender dug of common nurse.
and pp. of dig; as, they dug a ditch; a ditch was dug.
duke: In Great Britain, one of the highest order of nobility; a title of honor or nobility next below the princes; as the Duke of Bedford or of Cornwall.
In some countries on the Continent, a sovereign prince, without the title of king; as the Duke of Holstein, of Savoy, of Parma.
A chief; a prince; as the dukes of Edom. Genesis 36:15-
dukedom: The seignory or possessions of a duke; the territory of a duke.
The title or quality of a duke.
dulcet: Sweet to the taste; luscious. She tempers dulcet creams.
Sweet to the ear; melodious; harmonious; as dulcet sounds; dulcet symphonies.
dulcification: The act of sweetening; the act of freeing from acidity, saltness or acrimony.
dulcified: Sweetened; purified from salts. Dulcified spirits, a term formerly applied to the different ethers; as dulcified spirits of niter and vitriol, nitric and sulphuric ethers.
dulcify: To sweeten; to free from acidity, saltness or acrimony.
dulcimer: An instrument of music played by striking brass wires with little sticks. Daniel 3:5.
dulia: An inferior kind of worship or adoration.
dull: Stupid; doltish; blockish; slow of understanding; as a lad of dull genius.
Heavy; sluggish; without life or spirit; as a surfeit leaves a man very dull.
Slow of motion; sluggish; as a dull stream.
Slow of hearing or seeing; as dull of hearing; dull of seeing.
Slow to learn or comprehend; unready; awkward; as a dull scholar.
dull-head: A person of dull understanding; a dolt; a blockhead.
dullard: A stupid person; a dot; a blockhead; a dunce.
dullness: Stupidity; slowness of comprehension; weakness of intellect; indocility; as the dullness of a student.
Want of quick perception or eager desire.
dully: Stupidity; slowly; sluggishly; without life or spirit.
duly: Properly; fitly; in a suitable or becoming manner; as, let the subject be duly considered.
Regularly; at the proper time; as, a man duly attended church with his family.
dumb: Mute; silent; not speaking. I was dumb with silence; I held my peace. Psalm 39:2.

Destitute of the power of speech; unable to utter articulate sounds; as the dumb brutes. The asylum at Hartford in Connecticut was the first institution in America for teaching the deaf and dumb to read and write.
Mute; not using or accompanied with speech; as a dumb show; dumb signs. To strike dumb, is to confound; to astonish; to render silent by astonishment; or it may be, to deprive of the power of speech.
dumbly: Mutely; silently; without words or speech.
dumbness: Muteness; silence or holding the peace; omission of speech. This is voluntary dumbness.
In capacity to speak; inability to articulate sounds. This is involuntary dumbness.
dump: A dull gloomy state of the mind; sadness; melancholy; sorrow; heaviness of heart. In doleful dumps.
dumpish: Dull; stupid; sad; melancholy; depressed in spirits; as, he lives a dumpish life.
dumpishness: A state of being dull, heavy and moping.
dumpling: A kind of pudding or mass of paste in cookery; usually, a cover of paste inclosing an apple and boiled, called apple-dumpling.
dun: Of a dark color; of a color partaking of a brown and black; of a dull brown color; swarthy.
Dark; gloomy. In the dun air sublime.
To cure, as fish, in a manner to give them a dun color. ]

Literally, to clamor for payment of a debt. Hence, to urge for payment; to demand a debt in a pressing manner; to urge for payment with importunity. But in common usage, dun is often used in a milder sense, and signifies to call for, or ask for payment.
To urge importunately, in a general sense, but not an elegant word.
dunce: Duns. A person of weak intellects; a dullard; a dolt; a thickskull. I never knew this town without dunces of figure.
dunder: Lees; dregs; a word used in Jamaica. The use of dunder in the making of run answers the purpose of yeast int he fermentation of flour.
dun-fish: Codfish cured in a particular manner.
dungeon: A close prison; or a deep, dark place of confinement. And in a dungeon deep. They brought Joseph hastily out of the dungeon. Genesis 41:14.
A subterraneous place of close confinement.
dungfork: A fork used to throw dung from a stable or into a cart, or to spread it over land.
dunghill: Any mean situation or condition. He lifteth the beggar from the dunghill.7 Samuel 2:8.
A term of reproach for a man meanly born.
Sprung from the dunghill; mean; low; base; vile.
dungyard: A yard or inclosure where dung is collected.
dunlin: A fowl, a species of sandpiper.
dunnage: Faggots, boughs or loose wood laid on the bottom of a ship to raise heavy goods above the bottom.
dunned: Importuned to pay a dept; urged.
dunner: One employed in soliciting the payment of debts.
dunning: Urging for payment of a debt, or for the grant of some favor, or for the obtaining any request; importuning.

or n. The operation of curing codfish, in such a manner as to give it a particular color and quality. Fish for dunning are caught early in spring, and often in February. At the Isles of Shoals, off Portsmouth, in New Hampshire, the cod are taken in deep water, split and slack-salted; then laid in a pile for two or three months, in a dark store, covered, for the greatest part of the time, with salt-hay or eel-grass, and pressed with some weight. In April or May, they are opened and piled again as close as possible in the same dark store, till July or August, when they are fit for use.
dunnish: Inclined to a dun color; somewhat dun.
duodecimo: Having or consisting of twelve leaves to a sheet; as a book of duodecimo form or size.
A book in which a sheet is folded into twelve leaves.
duodenum: The first of the small intestines.
duoliteral: Consisting of two letters only; biliteral.
dupe: A person who is deceived; or one easily led astray by his credulity; as the dupe of a party.
To deceive; to trick; to mislead by imposing on ones credulity; as, to be duped by flattery.
dupion: A double cocoon, formed by two or more silk-worms.
duple: Double. Duple ratio is that of 2 to 1, 8 to 4
duplicate: Double; twofold. Duplicate proportion or ratio, is the proportion or ratio of squares. Thus in geometrical proportion, the first term to the third is said to be in a duplicate ratio of the first to the second, or as its square is to the square of the second. Thus in 2. 4. 8. 16., the ratio of 2 to 8 is a duplicate of that of 2 to 4, or as the square of 2 is to the square of
Another corresponding to the first; or a second thing of the same kind.
A copy; a transcript. Thus a second letter or bill of exchange exactly like the first is called a duplicate.
duplication: The act of doubling; the multiplication of a number by 2.
A folding; a doubling; also, a fold; as the duplication of a membrane.
duplicature: A doubling; a fold. In anatomy, the fold of a membrane or vessel.
duplicity: Doubleness of heart or speech; the act or practice of exhibiting a different or contrary conduct, or uttering different or contrary sentiments, at different times, in relation to the same thing; or the act of dissembling ones real opinions for the purpose of concealing them and misleading persons in the conversation and intercourse of life; double-dealing; dissimulation; deceit.
In law, duplicity is the pleading of two or more distinct matters or single pleas.
durability: The power of lasting or continuing, in any given state, without perishing; as the durability of cedar or oak timber; the durability of animal and vegetable life is very limited.
durable: 2. Having the quality of lasting or continuing long in being, without perishing or wearing out as durable timber; durable cloth; durable happiness.
durableness: Power of lasting; durability; as the durableness of honest fame.
durably: In a lasting manner; with long continuance.
durance: Imprisonment; restraint of the person; custody of the jailer.
durant: A glazed woolen stuff; called by some everlasting.
duration: Continuance in time; length or extension of existence, indefinitely; as the duration of life; the duration of a partnership; the duration of any given period of time; everlasting duration. This holding on or continuance of time is divided by us arbitrarily into certain portions, as minutes, hours and days; or it is measured by a succession of events, as by the diurnal and annual revolutions of the earth, or any other succession; and the interval between two events is called a part of duration. This interval may be of any indefinite length, a minute or a century.
dure: To last; to hold on in time or being; to continue; to endure.
duress: Literally, hardship; henceTechnically, duress, in law, is of two kinds; duress of imprisonment, which is imprisonment or restraint of personal liberty; and duress by menaces or threats when a person is threatened with loss of life or limb. Fear of battery is no duress. Duress then is imprisonment or threats intended to compel a person to do a legal act, as to execute a deed; or to commit an offense; in which cases the act is voidable or excusable.
during: Of dure. Continuing; lasting; holding on; as during life, that is, life continuing; during our earthly pilgrimage; during the space of a year; during this or that. These phrases are the case absolute, or independent clauses; durante vita, durante hoc.
durra: A kind of millet, cultivated in North Africa.
duse: A demon or evil spirit. Quosdam daemones quos dusios Galli nuncupant. August. De Civ. Dei, 15. 23. What the duse is the matter? The duse is in you.
dusk: Tending to darkness, or moderately dark.
Tending to a dark or black color; moderately black.
A tending to darkness; incipient or imperfect obscurity; a middle degree between light and darkness; twilight; as the dusk of the evening.
Tendency to a black color; darkness of color. Whose dusk set off the whiteness of the skin.
duskily: With partial darkness; with a tendency to blackness or darkness.
duskiness: Incipient or partial darkness; a slight or moderate degree of darkness or blackness.
duskish: Moderately dusky; partially obscure; slightly dark or black; as duskish smoke. Duskish tincture.
dusky: Partially dark or obscure; not luminous; as a dusky valley. A dusky torch.
Tending to blackness in color; partially black; dark-colored; not bright; as a dusky brown. Dusky clouds.
Gloomy; sad. This dusky scene of horror.
Intellectually clouded; as a dusky sprite.&
dust: Fine dry particles of earth or other matter, so attenuated that it may be raised and wafted by the wind; powder; as clouds of dust and seas of blood.
Fine dry particles of earth; fine earth. The peacock warmeth her eggs in the dust. Job 39:13-14.
Earth; unorganized earthy matter. Dust thou art, and to dust shalt thou return. Genesis 3:19.
The grave. For now shall I sleep in the dust. Job 7:27.
A low condition. God raiseth the poor out of the dust. 7 Samuel 2:8.
dust-brush: A brush for cleaning rooms and furniture.
duster: An utensil to clear from dust; also, a sieve.
dust-man: One whose employment is to carry away dirt and filth.
dusty: Filled, covered or sprinkled with dust; clouded with dust.
Like dust; of the color of dust; as a dusty white; a dusty red.
dutch: The people of Holland; also, their language.
Pertaining to Holland, or to its inhabitants.
duteous: Performing that which is due, or that which law, justice or propriety requires; obedient; respectful to those who have natural or legal authority to require service or duty; as a duteous child or subject.
Obedient; obsequious; in a good or bad sense. Duteous to the vices of thy mistress.
Enjoined by duty, or by the relation of one to another; as duteous ties.
dutiable: Subject to the imposition of duty or customs; as dutiable goods.
dutiful: Performing the duties or obligations required by law, justice or propriety; obedient; submissive to natural or legal superiors; respectful; as a dutiful son or daughter; a dutiful ward or servant; a dutiful subject.
Expressive of respect or a sense of duty; respectful; reverential; required by duty; as dutiful reverence; dutiful attentions.
dutifully: In a dutiful manner; with a regard to duty; obediently; submissively; reverently; respectfully.
dutifulness: Obedience; submission to just authority; habitual performance of duty; as dutifulness to parents.
duty: That which a person owes to another; that which a person is bound, by any natural, moral or legal obligation, to pay, do or perform. Obedience to princes, magistrates and the laws is the duty of every citizen and subject; obedience, respect and kindness to parents are duties of children; fidelity to friends is a duty; reverence, obedience and prayer to God are indispensable duties; the government and religious instruction of children are duties of parents which they cannot neglect without guilt.
Forbearance of that which is forbid by morality, law, justice or propriety. It is our duty to refrain from lewdness, intemperance, profaneness and injustice.
Act of reverence or respect. They both did duty to their lady.
The business of a soldier or marine on guard; as, the company is on duty. It is applied also to other services or labor.
duumvir: One of two Roman officers or magistrates united int he same public functions.
duumviral: Pertaining to the duumvirs or duumvirate of Rome.
duumvirate: The union of two men in the same office; or the office, dignity or government of two men thus associated; as in ancient Rome.
dwale: In heraldry, a sable or black color.
The deadly nightshade, a plant or a sleepy potion.
dwarf: A general name for an animal or plant which is much below the ordinary size of the species or kind. A man that never grows beyond two or three feet in highth, is a dwarf. This word when used alone usually refers to the human species, but sometimes to other animals. When it is applied to plants, it is more generally used in composition; as a dwarf-tree; dwarf-elder.
An attendant on a lady or knight in romances.
To hinder from growing to the natural size; to lessen; to make or keep small.
dwarfish: Like a dwarf; below the common stature or size; very small; low; petty; despicable; as a dwarfish animal; a dwarfish shrub.
dwarfishness: Smallness of stature; littleness of size.
dwell: pret. dwelled, usually contracted into dwelt.

To abide as a permanent resident, or to inhabit for a time; to live in a place; to have aé é habitation for some time or permanence. God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem. Genesis 9:27. Dwell imports a residence of some continuance. We use abide for the resting of a night or an hour; but we never say, he dwelt in a place a day or a night. Dwell may signify a residence for life or for a much shorter period, but not for a day.
